entries.title: White Sox catcher Kyle Teel finally set to begin rehab assignment Tuesday
entries.summary: <p>Catcher <b>Kyle Teel</b> finally will begin a rehab assignment Tuesday with Triple-A Charlotte, the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="_blank" >White Sox</a> said Sunday.</p><p>Teel suffered a Grade 2 strain of his right hamstring March 10, getting hurt while running out a double for Team Italy in a victory against Team USA at the World Baseball Classic. He reportedly was to be sent to Charlotte two weeks ago, but that was pushed back after he tweaked the injury.</p><p>Teel, 24, was all smiles after the Sox’ 2-1 victory against the Mariners.</p><p>‘‘I feel really good right now, just excited to get playing again,’’ Teel said. ‘‘It’s been a minute, so just looking forward to it.</p><p>‘‘This team is electric right now — the attitude, how hard everyone’s playing and the success that we’re having. I can’t wait to be a part of it and help contribute.’’</p><p>Teel resumed running the bases during workouts this weekend at Rate Field. General manager <b>Chris Getz</b> earlier had hinted that might be the final hurdle for Teel before a rehab assignment.</p><p>No timeline has been set for Teel’s return to the Sox. He said he thinks he’ll be behind the plate Tuesday at Charlotte, then serve as the designated hitter in his next game.</p><p>‘‘Think I’m only catching five [innings] the first game, but we’ll just go and see how it plays out,’’ Teel said.</p><p>Teel said he has had no trouble catching and hitting for some time, ‘‘but the problem was always the running.’’</p><p>Teel, who was acquired from the Red Sox in the <b>Garrett Crochet</b> trade in December 2024, hit .273 with eight home runs and 35 RBI and walked 37 times in 297 plate appearances for the Sox last season.</p><h3>Mune’s defense</h3><p>Rookie <b>Munetaka Murakami</b> settled for a single and an intentional walk Sunday and remained at 15 homers. Meanwhile, the Yankees’ <b>Aaron Judge</b> and the Phillies’ <b>Kyle Schwarber</b> went deep to pull ahead of him for the major-league lead with 16.</p><p>While everyone focuses on the long balls, Sox bench coach <b>Walker McKinven</b> said the staff is noticing Murakami’s continued improvement at first base. He has committed only two errors and is fine-tuning other elements of the position.</p><p>‘‘On the whole, he’s been steadily rising as a defender and has been a very trustworthy defender over there,’’ McKinven said.</p><p>What does he like about Murakami’s work?</p><p>‘‘[His] footwork around the bag,’’ McKinven said. ‘‘He’s been excellent with picking baseballs at the bag, something that has gotten brushed under the rug.’’</p><p>Murakami, however, still is getting used to defensive shifts that might take him far off the bag.</p><p>‘‘We want to get him comfortable with his space off the bag and trusting he can get back,’’ McKinven said.</p><h3>Cold ‘Dumper’</h3><p>The Sox kept All-Star catcher <b>Cal Raleigh</b> on ice in their series victory against the Mariners.</p><p>Raleigh hit 60 homers last season to set records for a catcher and a switch-hitter, but he is hitless in his last 36 plate appearances.</p><p>Right-hander <b>Davis Martin</b> walked Raleigh with two outs in the sixth inning Sunday, but that was the only time he reached base in the series.</p><p>Sox outfielder <b>Jarred Kelenic</b> claims to have given the 6-2, 235-pound Raleigh the nickname ‘‘Big Dumper’’ when he played for the Mariners. It’s a quip about the size of Raleigh’s backside.</p><p>‘‘Probably the first one to call him that,’’ Kelenic said. ‘‘I’m not going to take too much credit for it, but he’s earned everything that he’s gotten.’’</p><div class="PageListL" data-module data-hide-authors="true" data-hide-categories="true" data-hide-dates="true" data-show-loadmore="true" ><a class="AnchorLink" id="list-4a0005" name="list-4a0005"></a><div class="PageList-header" > <div class="PageList-header-title-wrap" > <div class="PageList-header-title"> <a class="" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox" target="{borderColor=#dfdfdf, pageNumber=1, inverseColors=false, footerBgColor=#000000, bodyFont=font-3, items=[{publishDate=May 10, 2026 06:26 PM, type=Image, category=[{items=[White Sox]}], anchorId=promo-2a0000, _styledTemplate=null, wordCount=610, imageAlignment=null, media=[{image={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/06/b8/afdca1c644bd95945f4965420787/mariners-white-sox-baseball-1.jpg, width=5582, height=3721}, narrowImage={src=https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/06/b8/afdca1c644bd95945f4965420787/mariners-white-sox-baseball-1.jpg, width=5582, height=3721}, ampLink=null, _styledTemplate=null, _template=/image/Image.hbs, alt=Mariners White Sox Baseball}], categoryUrl=https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox, isFeatured=false, title=Gamble pays off as White Sox rally in eighth to win series vs. Mariners, target=null, primaryMediaType=null, url=https://chicago.suntimes.com/white-sox/2026/05/10/white-sox-mariners-drew-romo-davis-martin-randal-grichuk-miguel-vargas-justin-jirschele-randy-arozarena-seranthony-dominguez, ampLink=null, description=[{items=[Randy Arozarena raced in to catch Miguel Vargas’ fly, which was measured at 243 feet by Statcast. entries.title: Horoscope for Monday, May 11, 2026
entries.summary: <h2>Moon Alert</h2> <p>There are no restrictions to shopping or important decisions today. The moon is in Pisces.</p><h2>Aries (March 21-April 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> The vibes are positive today, and they're great for doing research. Dig deep if you want answers to questions that concern you because you will likely find something helpful. Issues related to cash flow, money and purchases also might be on your mind. Ka-ching!</p><h2>Taurus (April 20-May 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Get out and enjoy the company of others because this is a marvelous day to schmooze with friends! In particular, it's a great day to interact with groups, clubs and organizations because today you can talk to anyone and dazzle them. (You might buy something attractive for yourself and loved ones.)</p><h2>Gemini (May 21-June 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You're high-visibility today! Because people notice you more than usual, you might take this opportunity to speak up about something. (After all, you do have their attention.) You might want to encourage others to work together or cooperate to achieve a goal. (It could be anything.)</p><h2>Cancer (June 21-July 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Do something different today because you want to shake things up a little. Travel or a chance to learn something new about where you live — something different and interesting — will please you. You might also talk to people who have interesting stories to tell. Expand your world!</p><h2>Leo (July 23-Aug. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is a good day for discussions about shared property, taxes, debt and financial matters. If someone owes you money, this is a good day to ask for it because your reputation is solid. People admire you, and they will listen to what you have to say.</p><h2>Virgo (Aug. 23-Sept. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> You make a great impression on bosses, parents and VIPs today. In fact, some of you might strike up a flirtation or a romance with a boss or someone in a position of authority. Meanwhile, when dealing with spouses, partners and close friends, you might have to go more than halfway. (This is no biggie.)</p><h2>Libra (Sept. 23-Oct. 22)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is an excellent day for project planning related to your job or any kind of work that interests you. It's also a solid day to look for a job. Ask for increased resources, a bigger budget, or extra help because you might get it. (Be patient with partners and friends.)</p><h2>Scorpio (Oct. 23-Nov. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> It's Monday, and you're ready to work and get things done. Nevertheless, it's also the perfect day to socialize with others because people are friendly. Social outings, sports events and fun times with kids will appeal. It's a winning day!</p><h2>Sagittarius (Nov. 22-Dec. 21)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Your privacy matters to you today, which is why you might want to relax at home and play things low-key. Nevertheless, you will still be productive at some point because you're full of clever ideas. Competitive sports will appeal to you.</p><h2>Capricorn (Dec. 22-Jan. 19)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> This is the perfect day to be with your friends and people you love. Enjoy good times with neighbors, relatives and siblings because you'll have an easygoing rapport with everyone. This is also a fun-loving, entertaining day. Stock the fridge.</p><h2>Aquarius (Jan. 20-Feb. 18)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-7b0000" name="html-embed-module-7b0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>positive</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Financial plans, budget plans and project planning might appeal to you today. This could relate to your job, home and family, or real estate. Don't hesitate to speak up about what you want because this is an excellent day to verbalize your feelings.</p><h2>Pisces (Feb. 19-March 20)</h2> <p></p> <div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="HtmlModule"><a class="AnchorLink" id="html-embed-module-6f0000" name="html-embed-module-6f0000"></a><div class="HoroscopeStarbox HoroscopeStarbox_five"> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_container"> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> <svg class="HoroscopeStar"><use xlink:href="#icon-star"></use></svg> </div> <div class="HoroscopeStarbox_description"> <p>A <b>dynamic</b> day</p> </div> </div> <style> .HoroscopeStarbox { background: #f8f8f8; display: flex; align-items: center; } .HoroscopeStarbox_container { display: grid; grid-gap: 5px; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr 1fr; padding: 20px; width: 40%; min-width: 160px; max-width: 200px; } .HoroscopeStarbox_description { padding: 20px; font-family: var(--font-1); font-size: var(--body-2); } .HoroscopeStarbox_container svg { object-fit: cover; aspect-ratio: 1 / 1; width: 100%; } </style> </div> </div><p> Today the moon is in your sign, dancing beautifully with chatty Mercury, making this a great day to talk to friends and acquaintances. It's also a good day to share news through letters, phone calls, messages or texts. Keep scrolling.</p><h2>If today is your birthday</h2><p>Actress Mary Elizabeth Ellis (1979) shares your birthday today. You are innovative, imaginative, unorthodox, but determined. This is because you are exceptionally self-disciplined. Good news! This year you reap the benefits of all your hard work. Expect power and leadership. You might get a promotion, an award, or kudos of some kind. The spotlight will find you! </p>
entries.author: Georgia Nicols

entries.title: $10,000 reward offered following West Side double murder of Uber driver and high school basketball player
entries.summary: <p>A $10,000 reward was being offered for information leading to an arrest in the double murder of an Uber driver and a high school basketball player. </p><p>Jassen Cho, 38, and Damarion Johnson, 18, were driving in the <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/05/08/high-school-senior-and-man-38-gunned-down-in-garfield-park-neighborhood" target="_blank" >200 block of North Homan Avenue </a> around 8:30 p.m. Thursday when someone inside a gray SUV fired at them, Chicago police said.</p><p>Cho was pronounced dead at Mount Sinai Hospital at 8:54 p.m., while Johnson died at 8:57 p.m. at Stroger Hospital, both from multiple gunshot wounds, according to the Cook County medical examiner’s office, which ruled their deaths homicides.</p><p>“This was a senseless act of violence that took the lives of two individuals and devastated their families," <b> </b>said Paul Rutherford, executive director of Cook County Crime Stoppers in a statement. "Someone in the community knows who is responsible. We urge anyone with information, no matter how small it may seem, to come forward anonymously," said Rutherford in the statement. "Your tip could help bring justice to the victims and help prevent additional violence in our neighborhoods."<br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Back of the Yards shooting leaves man, 30, seriously hurt
entries.summary: <p>Police are looking for a gunman who opened fire through a door of a Back of the Yards home and shot a 30-year-old man, seriously injuring him early Monday. </p><p>It happened about 4:50 a.m. in the 4600 block of South Winchester Avenue, according to police. </p><p>The 30-year-old victim suffered gunshot wounds to the head and shoulder and was taken to Stroger Hospital in serious condition, police said,. </p><p>No arrests have been made. </p><p>Area 1 detectives are investigating. <br></p>
entries.author: Sun-Times Wire

entries.title: Hypocritical anti-immigrant Americans rely on immigrants, too
entries.summary: <p>In late 2025, UCLA released a <a class="Link" href="https://latino.ucla.edu/research/latino-immigrant-labor-red-blue-states/" target="_blank" >study</a> that looked at Latino immigrant labor employment across every major red and blue state across the country. Researchers found that Texas and Florida, among the loudest anti-immigration states, depend on immigrant labor at about the roughly the same rates as California and more than New York. In other words, red states can't function without the very people they say they want to deport.</p><p>Red state conservatives attack immigrants publicly, so no one looks too closely at their own behavior. As Shakespeare once wrote: "The lady doth protest too much, methinks."</p><p>A good example of someone "protesting too much" has been Donald Trump, who has repeatedly attacked undocumented migrants as <a class="Link" href="https://www.reuters.com/world/us/trump-expected-highlight-murder-michigan-woman-immigration-speech-2024-04-02/" target="_blank" >“animals”</a> who "poison the blood of our country." </p><p>Given those types of comments, it is more than ironic that the Trump Tower in Manhattan was built by employing undocumented <a class="Link" href="https://catherwood.library.cornell.edu/wit/trump-paid-over-1-million-in-labor-settlement-documents-reveal/" target="_blank" >Polish workers in 1980s</a>, while Trump was telling Americans that immigrants were stealing their jobs. Trump's <a class="Link" href="https://www.nytimes.com/2018/12/06/us/trump-bedminster-golf-undocumented-workers.html" target="_blank" >golf courses</a> hired undocumented workers for decades. Of course, Trump will deny all of these facts as "fake news."</p><p>Trump and MAGA need you to hate immigrants, so you don't notice they're the ones hiring immigrants. The louder the outrage, the bigger the secret they're hiding from the public.</p><p>By the way, aren't we still waiting for millions of more documents to be released from the Epstein files?</p><p><i>Bob Chimis, Elmwood Park</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-f30000" name="module-f30000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>Give us your take</h3><br>Send letters to the editor to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. To be considered for publication, letters must include your full name, your neighborhood or hometown and a phone number for verification purposes. Letters should be a maximum of approximately 375 words.</div> </div><p></p><h3>Abusive cops are among repeat violent offenders who dodge accountability</h3><p>I strongly agree with many of the points raised by retired<i> </i>Riverside Police Chief <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/other-views/2026/05/01/police-officers-swedish-hospital-shooting-safe-t-act-electronic-monitoring-tom-weitzel" target="_blank" >Tom Weitzel</a> in his recent op-ed regarding the public view of policing.</p><p>But I feel an important issue needs to be brought into the mix. When he speaks of "violent offenders being cycled through the system with no meaningful consequences," it is hard not to think of those notorious instances where it was police brutality, ending with fatalities in some cases, that resulted in no meaningful consequences to the actors.</p><p>People need to be reassured that those rare incidents of excessive and violent behavior will result in serious punishment and not that justice favors the powerful.</p><p><i>Victor Skade, Brookfield</i></p><h3>Breaking Chicago’s flooding cycle</h3><p> As severe storms increase in intensity and frequency, Chicago is being forced to fight today’s storms with yesterday’s tools, and residents are paying the price with their health, homes and peace of mind. Using federal disaster recovery dollars received last year, the city must reimagine Chicago’s response to severe downpours. Chicagoans deserve a city that meets this moment. </p><p>Tens of thousands of homes flooded in 2023, leaving behind not only costly repairs for the city and residents but also serious health risks such as mold, the Sun-Times’ Brett Chase <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/the-watchdogs/2026/04/14/chicago-flooding-mold-health-climate-weather-rain-planning-soaked" target="_blank" >recently reported</a>. The aftermath of flooding lingers for years, affecting residents’ health, finances and well-being. </p><p>As more severe weather events bring heavier rainfall, this cycle is becoming more common and more expensive.  But the deeper problem is that much of Chicago’s stormwater system was never designed for the scale of storms we are now experiencing, and the city’s green places are far too few to naturally absorb excess water. Fixing that will require a more comprehensive approach to infrastructure that reduces flood risk and centers on community health and resilience.  </p><p>We have learned the hard way that no single solution can handle today’s storms. Traditional “gray” infrastructure — such as pipes, pumps and tunnels — remains essential, but our system is failing with the climate realities we face today.</p><p>Natural systems — from wetlands and urban tree canopies to rain gardens and parks — can slow, store and absorb stormwater before it overwhelms pipes and pavement.  <a class="Link" href="https://www.nature.org/en-us/about-us/who-we-are/how-we-work/policy/natures-dividends/" target="_blank" >Research from The Nature Conservancy,</a> drawing on nearly 1,500 studies, shows these nature-based solutions can also deliver strong economic returns by reducing flood damage while improving air and water quality, cooling neighborhoods and strengthening communities. The best results come from sustained investment in both green and gray solutions, designed to work together. </p><p>Chicago should take action now to invest in flood recovery and resilience with the federal dollars already on hand. This presents a rare opportunity, not just to rebuild what was damaged, but to rethink how we reduce risk going forward. If we invest in a more complete strategy, we can begin to break the cycle and design a system not for the past but for the future. </p><p>Georgie Geraghty, Illinois executive director and Midwest partner, <a class="Link" href="https://www.nature.org/en-us/about-us/who-we-are/our-people/georgie-geraghty/" target="_blank" ><i>The Nature Conservancy</i></a></p><h3>Wheels of injustice </h3><p>Apparently, the appropriate response to comply with a field sobriety test is "screw you." It obviously worked for the Cook County Karen — Board of Review Commissioner Samantha Steele — who was <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/05/05/samantha-steele-chicago-cook-county-board-of-review-crime-dui-drunk" >acquitted</a> by a judge who said evidence only showed "suspicion of intoxication."</p><p>Steele was driving erratically, hit a parked car, had an open bottle of wine in her car and refused to cooperate with police, who noticed her slurring in the 2024 incident.</p><p>What more did the judge need? The judge set a bad precedent. Be belligerent, uncooperative, make up a dog-ate-my-homework excuse and get off. We need our judicial system to apply common sense to clear-cut cases.</p><p><i>Steven Fortuna, Naperville</i></p><h3>Honoring nurses </h3> <p>I would like to thank and recognize all nurses during this year's <a class="Link" href="https://www.usatoday.com/story/money/2026/05/06/national-nurses-week-2026-deals/89944474007/" target="_blank" >Nurse’s Week</a>, which started last Wednesday and ends Tuesday.</p><p>I am retired after 45 years of hospital nursing. I look back on my career with gratitude and satisfaction. There is nothing in the world like caring for a patient and helping patients and their families during a stressful time.</p><p>These days, nurses are having a difficult time with a nursing shortage and the devaluation of medical science by our current federal administration. Please thank the nurses in your life, and encourage students with interest in people and science to join this rewarding profession.</p><p><i>Patricia Warman, Des Plaines</i></p>
entries.author: Letters to the Editor

entries.title: Dear Abby: Siblings endured unimaginable horrors at home
entries.summary: <p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> I'm the youngest of a family of three boys and two girls. The only person I had any connection with growing up was my sister "Sara." We were both treated the same way by our mother. We were not wanted. And I always knew Sara was sad about something else.</p><p>Our mother's discipline took the form of when something happened, she'd line us all up and tell us if the person who did it didn't admit it, she was going to whip us all. Sara usually got upset, and I couldn't take it, so I'd end up taking the beating. So while Mom screamed about why I did something, I honestly didn't know what had been done, and she beat me until she wore herself out.</p><p>Now that I'm an adult, I realize that the one who did the deed probably realized all he had to do was keep quiet until I took the blame, and the others probably still thought I was the guilty party. I know he will never admit to anything. Sara's sadness was because she was being raped by that brother, and Mom never believed her.</p><p>I am dirt to the rest of the family. The others don't want anything to do with me. Must I just accept this, or can I change this? — <i>FAMILY DISASTER IN INDIANA</i></p><p><b>DEAR FAMILY DISASTER:</b> You and your siblings grew up in the very definition of a toxic environment. Your mother's abuse damaged everyone. While you cannot change your siblings' opinion of you, Sara can by speaking up about what really was going on in that house when you were all growing up. From reading your letter, it seems like a nightmare.</p><p>All of you could benefit from counseling for victims of abuse. If you are willing to get it, you definitely should. I would also encourage you to visit RAINN at rainn.org for additional support.</p><p><b>DEAR ABBY:</b> After my mother's death 10 years ago, my father moved in with me. He's 74 and in reasonably good health but has always struggled with depression and social anxiety. He refuses to take medication. Although there are many opportunities for him to have social contact, he's very critical of most family and friends.</p><p>Dad does have some positives. He exercises and helps me with cooking and my kids. Nevertheless, his depression creates such tension in the household that most of the time I avoid talking to him because I don't want to hear the negativity he is sure to spew. When I do talk to him, it is likely to turn into an argument, or he will insult me.</p><p>I am so exhausted. I know he can't live on his own, but I don't know what to do. He has been seen by doctors and mental health professionals, but he is not willing to engage. Please advise. — <i>GETTING TIRED IN NEW JERSEY</i></p><p><b>DEAR GETTING TIRED:</b> The next time you talk to your father and he argues or insults you, tell him you have had enough. Tell him that you can no longer handle the tension his dependency and depression create in your home and that he must either get the help he needs or find another place to live. Then set a date for him to comply or move.</p><p>If there is a senior center near where you live, you may be able to get assistance in dealing with your resistant dad. Your local Area Agency on Aging might also be able to offer guidance.</p><p><i>Dear Abby is written by Abigail Van Buren, also known as Jeanne Phillips, and was founded by her mother, Pauline Phillips. Contact Dear Abby at www.DearAbby.com or P.O. Box 69440, Los Angeles, CA 90069.</i></p><p>For an excellent guide to becoming a better conversationalist and a more sociable person, order "How to Be Popular." Send your name and mailing address, plus check or money order for $8 (U.S. funds) to: Dear Abby, Popularity Booklet, P.O. Box 446, Kings Mills, OH 45034-0446. (Shipping and handling are included in the price.)</p>
entries.author: Abigail Van Buren

entries.title: Lesson for every police officer: Find peace and quiet, before the job consumes you
entries.summary: <p>I knew early on I wanted a life of service.</p><p>I spent eight years in the United States Marine Corps before beginning my law enforcement career in 2005. That transition wasn’t a departure from service so much as a continuation of it — just in a different uniform, with different stakes and different kinds of calls for help. Today, I serve as a detective with the Joliet Police Department, working cases that require focus, patience and attention to detail.</p><p>Across both careers, I have seen what trauma and loss look like on a personal level. You learn how to manage it. You compartmentalize, stay focused and keep moving forward because the work demands it, and the people you serve are counting on you.</p><p>Over time, I realized something most people in this profession come to understand: The job will take everything you give it, if you let it.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-1b0000" name="module-1b0000"></a> <div class="RichTextSidebarModule-title">Commentary bug</div> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h2>Commentary</h2></div> </div><p>What stays with you is not one defining moment but the accumulation of long shifts, difficult calls and the responsibility that follows you home, whether you want it to or not.</p><h3>Restless nights, less patience</h3><p>For many in law enforcement and the military, that weight builds quietly. It shows up in restless nights, shorter patience and a constant sense of being “on,” even when the shift is over. Over time, that becomes normal in a way that is hard to explain outside the profession.</p> <p>For a long time, I accepted that as part of the job. Then I found something that helped me step away from it, even if only for a little while.</p><p>For me, that place is on the water.</p><p>When I am fishing, my focus shifts completely. I am not thinking about cases or past calls. I am thinking about what lure to use, where the fish might be and how the water is moving. It is simple, but that is the point. It forces my mind to be present in a way the job rarely allows.</p><p>A few summers ago, I had a moment that changed how I thought about that time. Sitting on the water, I realized I had no worries in that moment. No negative thoughts. No frustration — just calm that felt very different from the pace of daily life.</p><p>It made me realize how much that kind of reset matters, not just for me, but for others still carrying the same weight without enough outlets to release it.</p><p>That realization led me to Heroes on the Water and eventually to starting a chapter in the Chicago area. What we do is straightforward. We bring police officers, veterans, first responders and their families together for time on the water.</p><p>But what happens there goes beyond the activity itself. It creates space to slow down, be present and set aside, even briefly, the things we carry every day. That kind of space is rare in professions that rarely slow down.</p><h3>A big catch</h3><p>I have seen its impact in moments that might seem small from the outside but matter deeply to the people living them. A veteran trying something new after years away from it. An officer spending uninterrupted time with family without calls or schedules pulling them away. People laughing, relaxed and fully present in a way that is not always easy to reach in daily life.</p><p>One moment stands out: a Navy veteran and retired Chicago police officer who joined us, got into a kayak for the first time and caught her first fish. She spent the entire day smiling. It was simple, but it mattered. Because for a while, whatever she had been carrying was not the focus.</p><p>That is what this is about. Not escaping the realities of the job but making it possible to keep doing it without letting it take everything.</p><p>In both military and law enforcement, there is a mindset of pushing through. That mindset is necessary in critical moments, but it cannot be the only way forward in everyday life. There also has to be space to reset, reconnect and take care of the person behind the role before the weight becomes too heavy to carry alone.</p><p>I practice being more intentional with my time — prioritizing family, taking breaks to do the things I love and recognizing that stepping away is part of staying steady in the long run.</p><p>For others, it might look different. But the need is the same.</p><p>We all carry something. The difference is whether we have a place to put it down, even for a little while. For me, that place is on the water.</p><p>As National Police Week is observed, these stories matter because they speak to what is often unseen in this profession: the need for space to reset, reconnect and continue showing up for others without losing yourself in the process.</p><p><i> Jeremy Eaton is a detective with the Joliet Police Department and a U.S. Marine Corps veteran. He also founded the Heroes on the Water Chicago chapter, supporting veterans, first responders and their families through outdoor recreation and peer connection.</i></p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-c90000" name="module-c90000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody">Send letters to <a class="Link" href="mailto:letters@suntimes.com" target="_blank" >letters@suntimes.com</a>. More about <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/submitting-opinion-and-letters" >how to submit here</a>.</div> </div><p></p>
entries.author: Jeremy Eaton

entries.title: About 60% of Chicago arts workers earn less than $40,000
entries.summary: <p>Nearly two-thirds of arts workers in Chicago reported earning less than $40,000 annually in a <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagoartscensus.com/en/explore-the-data/" target="_blank" ><u>new survey</u></a> that takes stock of the sector. Those earnings fall below the city’s <a class="Link" href="https://data.census.gov/table/ACSDT1Y2021.B19301?q=Chicago+per+capita+income+2021." target="_blank" ><u>overall average income</u></a> per capita for the years surveyed.</p><p>The <a class="Link" href="https://www.chicagoartscensus.com/en/" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Arts Census</u></a> initially grew out of the COVID-19 pandemic and was organized by a coalition of arts workers seeking to put real numbers to their own experiences. More than 1,200 people who work across arts disciplines took the 120-question survey; the project received funding from major culture philanthropists including the Walder Foundation and the John D. and Catherine T. MacArthur Foundation.</p><p>The census also found that arts workers are significantly less likely to be homeowners than most Chicagoans, even though 44% of respondents report having begun or completed a master's degree program.</p><p>“We have all lived these data points, and so seeing them is not surprising,” said Kate Bowen, one of the census’ lead organizers. “But what feels exciting and hopefully feels powerful to folks is that it's together in one place, and it is reflecting what we've experienced as arts workers.”</p><p>The survey findings were released last month after years of analysis, which was done, in part, by data scientist Leonardo Figueiredo of the Rila Group. The respondents represent all 77 Chicago neighborhoods, although there were concentrations in areas like Logan Square.</p><p>Slightly more than half of the respondents identified as women and about 60% were white.</p><p>The findings arrive in a precarious moment for the arts. Funding for the field <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/city-hall/2025/11/05/chicago-dcase-cultural-affairs-budget-clinee-hedspeth-kenya-merritt-grants-arts" target="_blank" ><u>has been cut at the city level</u></a>, stagnant in <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2026/02/23/pritzker-budget-arts-funding-flat-illinois-2026-2027-grants-trump" target="_blank" ><u>the proposed state budget</u></a> and <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2025/05/06/nea-grants-trump-chicago-cultural-events" target="_blank" ><u>under attack federally</u></a>.</p><p>Still, local government officials have repeatedly touted the arts as a driver of Chicago’s economy. Last year, <a class="Link" href="https://www.choosechicago.com/press-media/press-releases/chicago-hotels-set-record-for-summer-room-demand/" target="_blank" ><u>Choose Chicago reported</u></a> a record-setting summer of tourism, citing the city’s “festivals, concerts, and Chicago’s world-renowned cultural and culinary scene” as key factors in attracting visitors. Plus, a report from the <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/economy/2025/10/15/chicago-loop-foot-traffic-post-pandemic-arts-culture-events" target="_blank" ><u>Chicago Loop Alliance</u></a> found that the arts have played a major role in drawing foot traffic Downtown after the pandemic.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-1b0000" name="image-1b0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b2ea62e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffc%2F71%2F95f9f77f441fb3069c8cc3863723%2Fmurals-101225-20.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b670f9f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffc%2F71%2F95f9f77f441fb3069c8cc3863723%2Fmurals-101225-20.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b2ea62e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ffc%2F71%2F95f9f77f441fb3069c8cc3863723%2Fmurals-101225-20.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The census organizers said this report gives the sector actual data to bring to conversations with policymakers and better advocate for the field, which has faced funding cuts in recent years.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>But an arts career lacks stability, artists reported in the survey. The census found that 88% of arts workers strongly believed their work was valuable. However, fewer than half felt that they had a strong sense of job security. And while nearly all of the respondents (95%) said they were compensated for their work in the field, only 57% said they typically receive payment promptly. About 37% said they are only occasionally or never paid on time.</p><p>“Artists are frequently [contract] employees, and I think it's part of the practice of the people who are paying artists, you aren't paid until after the project, so that means you are fronting your own money for that thing,” said Bowen, who is the executive director of ACRE, a Chicago nonprofit artist residency and exhibition program.</p><p>Bowen worries that if Chicago becomes unaffordable, the city's artistic talent will leave the sector or move elsewhere. “It means that the industry isn't going to have as much talent, as much of that economic driver,” she said.</p><p>Respondents completed the survey before Donald Trump’s reelection in 2024, so the impact of <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/arts-culture/2025/05/06/nea-grants-trump-chicago-cultural-events" target="_blank" ><u>cuts to national arts funding</u></a> are not reflected. But, the organizers say that as they have started to present the data, they continue to hear about the changes in arts funding, not only from the government, but also from foundations whose priorities have shifted in recent years.</p><p>“There's been a contracture there, and people are reprioritizing because there's a lot more emergencies that are not the arts,” said Bowen. “We understand that the arts feel like a luxury to folks when we're talking about people starving or losing their SNAP benefits. But I think what we're also saying is the arts is a really powerful group of people that can advocate alongside the rest of workers who also might benefit from things like SNAP.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-e90000" name="image-e90000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/f949c5f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2F0e%2F9328b6e54e6fa7142cb14e1f1283%2Flillstreet-1216250163.jpg data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9a1bc79/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F52%2F0e%2F9328b6e54e6fa7142cb14e1f1283%2Flillstreet-1216250163.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The survey organizers worry that if Chicago becomes too unaffordable, the city’s artistic talent will leave the sector or move elsewhere.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The census organizers said this report gives the sector actual data to conversations with policymakers.</p><p>“It might not be a surprise to see how low the incomes of many of our respondents are, but often, when we are going to policymakers or grant makers or folks with resources, we can tell them about our lived experience, but they often need numbers to back that up,” said Anthony Stepter, ACRE’s program director, who has worked closely on the census project.</p><p>“Now we have information about income, we have information about resources related to childcare, we have information about education and all sorts of things that people can use in a really practical and specific manner.”</p><p>Beyond advocating for direct funding for the arts at the city and state level, the census organizers say investments in affordable housing and public transportation are also investments in the city’s arts community.</p><p>“Artists and arts workers are an incredibly resilient group who want very badly to see things be better for each other, not just themselves,” said Bowen.</p><p>A series of live events is planned to present the findings. The group will also soon launch the report in Spanish.</p><p><i>Courtney Kueppers is an arts and culture reporter at WBEZ.</i></p>
entries.author: Courtney Kueppers | WBEZ

entries.title: Arlington Heights or Hammond, Indiana? What to know in the Bears' stadium saga
entries.summary: <p>A five-year stadium odyssey could finally come to a head this spring for the Chicago Bears.</p><p>The team has secured a sweetheart deal from Indiana lawmakers that would help them build a new dome in Hammond, leaving it up to Illinois legislators to decide on property tax incentives that could at least keep them in their home state, if not their home city.</p><p>Here’s what’s at stake with the political football sitting at the goal line as the clock winds down on the legislative session in Springfield.</p><h3><b>Why do the Bears want to move?</b></h3><p>To make more money. Soldier Field is the smallest stadium in the NFL, and the Bears rent it from the Chicago Park District. Owning a bigger dome would allow the team to sell more tickets and rake in more revenue from concerts and other events.</p><h3><b>Where could the Bears go?</b></h3><h3></h3><p>Arlington Heights or Hammond, Indiana, are the only choices at this point, according to the team. But they’ve waffled on their preferred destination over the past few years.</p><p>The Bears explored <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2023/6/11/23754606/bears-stadium-arlington-heights-naperville-chicago-odds" target="_blank" ><u>a bevy of options</u></a> across the suburbs and made a full-court press for a new lakefront dome near Soldier Field that was soundly rejected by Illinois lawmakers. Then they leaned heavily toward Arlington Heights but, absent legislative support, announced last fall they were seriously considering crossing the state line <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/04/28/chicago-bears-hammond-hams-escalating-rhetoric-hoosiers-hope-bears-stadium" target="_blank" ><u>to Indiana</u></a>.</p><h3><b>But they’re definitely leaving Chicago?</b></h3><p>Yes, barring a sudden change of heart. Mayor Brandon Johnson cheered alongside Bears president Kevin Warren for their lakefront proposal in 2024, but Gov. JB Pritzker and legislative leaders threw cold water on that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2024/04/23/bears-new-stadium-dome-lakefront-soldier-field" target="_blank" ><u>expensive pitch</u></a> before the Bears’ press conference even ended. It would’ve required about $900 million in taxpayer funding and at least as much in other infrastructure spending.</p><p>Cook County Board President Toni Preckwinkle and other local leaders wanted the Bears to build on the old Michael Reese Hospital site in Bronzeville, but the team says it’s too narrow for an NFL stadium, and they haven’t identified any other workable sites within city limits.</p><p>Johnson is still working on <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/05/05/mayor-brandon-johnson-bears-stadium-legislation-arlington-heights-kam-buckner" target="_blank" ><u>a Hail Mary pitch</u></a> to keep the Bears in the city, but he hasn’t built up much Springfield clout in his term.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div alt="Bears Pres. Kevin Warren and Bears Chairman George McCaskey listen to Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson as he speaks during a news conference about the proposed Bears stadium at the United Club at Soldier Field, Wednesday, April 24, 2024. | Pat Nabong/Sun-Times" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/92f6818/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6890x3867+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2F1a%2Fb376c0b04484a635d35516a3fa21%2Fbearsstadium-042524-8.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/c845811/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6890x3867+0+397/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2F1a%2Fb376c0b04484a635d35516a3fa21%2Fbearsstadium-042524-8.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/92f6818/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6890x3867+0+397/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fab%2F1a%2Fb376c0b04484a635d35516a3fa21%2Fbearsstadium-042524-8.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Bears President Kevin Warren and Bears Chairman George McCaskey listen to Chicago Mayor Brandon Johnson as he speaks during a news conference about the proposed Bears stadium at the United Club at Soldier Field, Wednesday, April 24, 2024. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Pat Nabong/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>Why would the Bears move to Arlington Heights?</b></h3><p>The team paid $197.2 million for the shuttered Arlington International Racecourse in 2023, and there’s no shortage of money-making opportunities across its 326 acres. A new stadium would take up less than half the site, which they’ve envisioned as a mixed-use development with retail, restaurants and residential areas.</p><p>It’s also closer to their Halas Hall training facility, which the Bears renovated in 2019 at a cost of <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/02/20/bears-nfl-halas-hall-practice-facility" target="_blank" ><u>more than $100 million</u></a>. And while Chicago is a Bears town across the board, a huge portion of their fan base lives in the northwest suburbs.</p><h3><b>So why haven’t they built a stadium in Arlington Heights already?</b></h3><p>Their property tax bill would be astronomical. The team already isn’t thrilled with <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/bears-stadium/2024/12/12/bears-stadium-arlington-property-tax-deal" target="_blank" ><u>the $3.6 million they’re paying</u></a> annually on the vacant Arlington site, and without a change to state law, they’d easily owe upwards of $150 million in property taxes each year when it’s fully developed.</p><p>That’s why they want Illinois lawmakers to pass so-called<a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/04/22/bears-stadium-arlington-heights-property-tax-relief-infrastructure-northwest-indiana" > megaproject legislation</a>, which allows companies to negotiate discounted payments in lieu of taxes (PILOT) with local taxing bodies, saving them hundreds of millions of dollars over the next four decades.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-6f0000" name="image-6f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e84b4f6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6400x3592+0+4/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2Fcb%2F5f414da578464535254a3f590921%2Farlington-park-aerialview1-20220906.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/a54e97e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6400x3592+0+4/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2Fcb%2F5f414da578464535254a3f590921%2Farlington-park-aerialview1-20220906.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a6d874/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6400x3592+0+4/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2Fcb%2F5f414da578464535254a3f590921%2Farlington-park-aerialview1-20220906.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="A rendering of an aerial view of the stadium site in Arlington Heights was released by the Chicago Bears in September of 2022" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a6d874/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6400x3592+0+4/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2Fcb%2F5f414da578464535254a3f590921%2Farlington-park-aerialview1-20220906.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8ec846c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6400x3592+0+4/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2Fcb%2F5f414da578464535254a3f590921%2Farlington-park-aerialview1-20220906.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7a6d874/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6400x3592+0+4/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F7e%2Fcb%2F5f414da578464535254a3f590921%2Farlington-park-aerialview1-20220906.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>A rendering of an aerial view of the stadium site in Arlington Heights was released by the Chicago Bears on Tuesday, Sept. 6, 2022.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided by Chicago Bears</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>What’s on the table in Indiana?</b></h3><p>A tantalizing deal for the McCaskey family. Hoosier lawmakers authorized a new stadium authority that could put at least a billion taxpayer dollars into a dome in Hammond, backed by a slew of new taxes on admissions, hotel stays, food and beverages, plus a toll hike.</p><p>The Bears, who have committed to spending $2 billion on stadium construction wherever they end up, would retain all revenue generated by the dome and have the option to buy it for a buck once 40-year bonds are paid off by taxpayers.</p><p>While many Springfield observers view the Indiana threat as a leverage play for the team to get what it wants in Illinois, the bottom line makes it a tempting proposition.</p><h3><b>So why haven’t they left for Indiana already?</b></h3><p>The Bears are still going through their due diligence evaluating the Hammond site at the Lost Marsh Golf Course. It’s built over an old industrial dump within sniffing distance of an oil refinery, and situated near a Superfund site flagged by environmental regulators for toxin-tainted soil.</p><p>Logistical hurdles aside, the team would also face public blowback for leaving behind not only Chicago but the state that has supported a franchise now valued at nearly $9 billion.</p><h3><b>Will the Bears get their megaproject legislation from Springfield?</b></h3><p>That’s the billion-dollar question. The megaproject bill that passed the Illinois House last month marked the first significant legislative win for the team after four fruitless years of lobbying Springfield, but it’ll have to be significantly overhauled to pass the Illinois Senate or become palatable for the Bears. The bill sponsored by state Rep. Kam Buckner, D-Chicago, included a number of late additions without consulting the team, Senate leaders or Pritzker’s office.</p><p>The main sticking point is a provision calling for half of PILOT payments to be directed toward property tax relief for average homeowners. The governor’s office says that relief would be <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/05/04/bears-stadium-property-tax-relief-pritzker-negligible" target="_blank" ><u>negligible</u></a>, and from the Bears’ perspective, it would wipe out much of their potential PILOT savings.</p><p>State Sen. Bill Cunningham, the South Side Democrat leading talks in his chamber, said he’s optimistic they’ll find common ground in the next few weeks.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-820000" name="image-820000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/7711221/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6569x3687+0+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2F1c%2F144a7e8c40758df794cac3cb76d0%2Fmasstransit-121725-008.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3d7e6a7/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6569x3687+0+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2F1c%2F144a7e8c40758df794cac3cb76d0%2Fmasstransit-121725-008.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/343f2c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6569x3687+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2F1c%2F144a7e8c40758df794cac3cb76d0%2Fmasstransit-121725-008.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Governor JB Pritzker (left) stands with Senate President Don Harmon (center) and House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch (right) during the signing ceremony for the Northern Illinois Transit Authority Act at Union Station in The Loop, Tuesday, Dec. 16, 2025. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/343f2c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6569x3687+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2F1c%2F144a7e8c40758df794cac3cb76d0%2Fmasstransit-121725-008.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0ff0742/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6569x3687+0+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2F1c%2F144a7e8c40758df794cac3cb76d0%2Fmasstransit-121725-008.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/343f2c8/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6569x3687+0+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F04%2F1c%2F144a7e8c40758df794cac3cb76d0%2Fmasstransit-121725-008.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Gov. JB Pritzker (left) stands with Senate President Don Harmon (center) and House Speaker Emanuel “Chris” Welch (right) during the signing ceremony for the Northern Illinois Transit Authority Act at Union Station in The Loop, Tuesday, Dec. 16, 2025. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Candace Dane Chambers/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>What are the political stakes?</b></h3><p>No Chicago legislator wants to be held responsible for letting the team leave the city. That’s also true for progressives, though they are wary of giving any help to a multi-billion dollar corporation while everyday costs rise for average residents. </p><p>Pritzker doesn’t want to be remembered as the governor who lost the Bears, and while he’s long pushed for megaproject incentives, he also doesn’t want to be viewed as securing a handout for a lucrative pro sports franchise.</p><p>Nor does Johnson want to go down as the mayor who lost the Bears, although he can pin much of the blame on his predecessor, Lori Lightfoot. The question for Johnson is whether his effort <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/05/05/mayor-brandon-johnson-bears-stadium-legislation-arlington-heights-kam-buckner" target="_blank" ><u>to tank the Arlington Heights deal</u></a> is worth pushing the Bears across the border.</p><h3><b>What will it cost taxpayers?</b></h3><p>Indiana taxpayers would be on the hook for a whole suite of new taxes to build the Bears a stadium, but it’s harder to pin down the full cost for Illinoisans.</p><p>An estimated $855 million in public dollars would be needed to install sewers, water mains, traffic upgrades and other infrastructure <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2025/10/01/bears-stadium-arlington-heights-cost-renderings" target="_blank" ><u>to support the Arlington Heights development</u></a>. But a lot of those projects have already been earmarked and will be done regardless of whether a dome gets built, Pritzker has argued.</p><p>Beyond that, megaproject legislation would save the Bears hundreds of millions of dollars that would otherwise be going to local school districts and other taxing bodies. But the officials from the team — and other big businesses that might be incentivized to make big investments and create jobs — are quick to point out the schools are missing out on even more money without big projects raising property values.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-8d0000" name="image-8d0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/41c3b70/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6117x3433+0+323/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F79%2Fe09a68e84fb3a6caf03f0ae3decf%2Fbears-260111-91-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/521a5fd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6117x3433+0+323/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F79%2Fe09a68e84fb3a6caf03f0ae3decf%2Fbears-260111-91-1.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/53331a6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6117x3433+0+323/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F79%2Fe09a68e84fb3a6caf03f0ae3decf%2Fbears-260111-91-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago Bears cornerback Nahshon Wright #26 hypes up the crowd after warm-ups before the Bears take on the Green Bay Packers in an NFC wild-card game at Soldier Field, Saturday, Jan. 10, 2026. " srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/53331a6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6117x3433+0+323/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F79%2Fe09a68e84fb3a6caf03f0ae3decf%2Fbears-260111-91-1.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/40c601b/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6117x3433+0+323/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F79%2Fe09a68e84fb3a6caf03f0ae3decf%2Fbears-260111-91-1.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/53331a6/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6117x3433+0+323/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F5f%2F79%2Fe09a68e84fb3a6caf03f0ae3decf%2Fbears-260111-91-1.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Onetime Bears cornerback Nahshon Wright, now with the New York Jets, hypes up the crowd after warm-ups before the Bears take on the Green Bay Packers in their NFC wild card game at Soldier Field Jan. 10. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><b>What will happen to Soldier Field?</b></h3><p>The Chicago Fire will play at Soldier Field until their new, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/03/chicago-fire-football-club-breaks-ground-650-million-stadium-78" target="_blank" ><u>privately funded stadium</u></a> is finished in the South Loop in 2028.</p><p>Soldier Field is still the city’s premier summer concert venue, and Johnson’s administration is looking <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/02/24/soldier-field-renovation-post-chicago-bears-era-illinois-general-assembly" target="_blank" ><u>to secure $630 million from the state</u></a> for renovations to keep it viable even without 10 or so Bears games on the calendar.</p><p>Taxpayers are still footing the bill for the field’s 2003 renovation, with some $467 million in bonds still outstanding.</p><h3><b>When will the Bears make a decision?</b></h3><p>Soon ... probably. Warren has said they’ll name their destination in <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/01/bears-president-kevin-warren-targets-late-spring-or-early-summer-for-stadium-decision" target="_blank" ><u>late spring or early summer</u></a>. That’s coming from the executive who previously said he wanted shovels in the ground by the end of 2024, and again in ‘25.</p><p>But with Indiana’s offer in hand — and under pressure from the NFL to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears/2026/04/29/bears-meet-with-nfl-committee-stadium-and-plan-to-do-so-again-in-may" >pick a lane</a> — the stadium saga finally appears to be nearing a climax. The team won’t make any announcement until after they see what they get from Illinois lawmakers, whose spring session concludes May 31.</p><p>Bills can still move in Springfield after that date, but with a higher threshold for passage that would suggest season-ticket holders should start mapping out their Indiana commutes.</p>
entries.author: Mitchell Armentrout

entries.title: Airbag thefts in Chicago are on the rise, as are repair wait times. Here’s what to know.
entries.summary: <p>In March, Kieran Degenaars woke up on a Saturday morning, glanced out the window and noticed a car parked on his street had been broken into the night before.</p><p>“I saw a car with a smashed window and glass all over the ground,” he recalled. “I was like, ‘Oh I feel so bad for that person.'”</p><p>A moment later, Degenaars, 26, realized he was staring at his own car.</p><p>“It clicked: ‘Oh my gosh, that's my car,’” he remembered saying to himself. Degenaars assumed his 2018 Honda Civic was targeted because he had left a pair of pricey work boots in the back seat. But when he inspected his car, Degenaars found only one thing was missing — the airbag tucked inside the steering wheel.</p><p>The smashed glass from the driver-side window and replacement parts cost about $3,300 to repair, $2,000 of which was covered by insurance, according to Degenaars. He said he was also forced to pay for more than $1,200 in rideshare trips while his car took nearly three weeks to be repaired.</p><p>“It was really frustrating,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2c0000" name="image-2c0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Degenaars isn’t alone. There’s been a recent uptick in <a class="Link" href="https://www.reddit.com/r/chicago/comments/1syyfuh/if_you_street_park_a_honda_crv_or_hrv_be_aware/" target="_blank" >car break-ins targeting airbags</a> across the Chicago area, according to experts and an analysis of Chicago Police Department community alerts.</p><p>It takes some burglars less than one minute to break into a car and steal the airbag from the driver’s seat to eventually sell on the black market.</p><p>According to retired CPD Deputy Superintendent Anthony Riccio, who previously oversaw a unit that monitored airbag theft, some burglars coordinate with local body shops based on models that are in high demand.</p><p>“The black market, it's very specific, because it's very vehicle specific,” Riccio said. “The body shop would say, ‘I need an air bag for this vehicle,’ and the person would go out, find that specific vehicle, break into it and steal the air bag.”</p><p>Sair Arapovic, who owns Royce Auto in Bensenville, said he has seen more than 60 cases of stolen airbags in the past three months alone, with the recent surge causing a repair backlog.</p><p>“The dealerships can only do so much work,” Arapovic said. “So, you know, some of these cars sit for two, three weeks, maybe even a month, month and a half until the parts arrive.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div
