class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sair Arapovic, owner of Royce Auto poses for a portrait at his shop in Bensenville, IL, on May 4, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>While a new airbag from a legitimate dealer can cost upwards of $1,000, according to The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration, used airbags on eBay typically range from $150 to $600. The online marketplace requires sellers with airbag listings to provide the unique vehicle identification number of the donor vehicle and certification by the Automotive Recyclers Association.</p><p>However, airbags have a serial number associated with a generic car model rather than a specific car. “There's no way to tie an airbag to a certain vehicle,” Arapovic said. “You can tie it to a vehicle type but not that vehicle.”</p><p>NHTSA raised the alarm about airbag thefts as early as 2017. In Chicago, <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/2023/6/9/23752106/catalytic-converter-thefts-platinum-palladium-rhodium-cutters-buyers-scrap-metal-decanning" target="_blank" ><u>widespread catalytic converter thefts</u></a> have received most of the attention, but in 2026, airbag thefts are increasingly prevalent.</p><p>When Degenaars contacted the police, he said officers told him several other car owners in the area had reported similar incidents. Degenaars’ insurance company also said they had recently received numerous calls about airbag theft.</p><p>Roscoe Village, Degenaars’ North Side neighborhood, sits right next to Lake View, the area with the highest number of airbag theft community alerts from December 2025 to April 2026.</p><p>Out of 151 different community alerts analyzed by the Sun-Times, 34 of them came from Lake View. The alerts don't capture every crime, but they show thefts happening across the city. The second highest number, 18, occurred in Humboldt Park on the West Side, and there were 14 airbag thefts reported in Grand Boulevard on the South Side.</p><h3>What’s fueling the airbag black market </h3><p>Demand for stolen airbags increased nationwide following the recall of approximately 67 million airbags that began a decade ago, according to NHTSA.</p><p>Takata, a major Japanese auto supplier, manufactured defective airbags that exploded, killing 28 people in the U.S. and injuring at least 400 others, according to a report on NHTSA’s website.</p><p>The recall began in 2016 and continued in phases through December 2019. Years later, the auto industry still hasn’t produced enough airbags to bounce back.</p><p>Some of the cars hardest hit by the Takata airbag recall and a <a class="Link" href="https://www.npr.org/2024/02/06/1229514020/honda-acura-recall-air-bag-civic-accord-crv" target="_blank" ><u>more recent recall</u></a> in 2024 are Honda and Acura models, which are often singled out by thieves.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-3e0000" name="image-3e0000"></a> class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Sair Arapovic, owner of Royce Auto, opens a new airbag , to be installed in a Honda with a stolen airbag, in Bensenville, IL, on May 4, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>When Chicago resident Kiki Dusina recently found her Honda CR-V’s airbag stolen, she went to a Honda dealership for repair. There, they told her about the shortage. Dusina now expects her car to sit at the dealership for a month or two while waiting for an airbag replacement.</p><p>“It's on back order,” Dusina said. “Which, of course, is why there's a black market to begin with.”</p><p>Dusina said the entire experience has felt “like a gut punch.”</p><p>So why not just make it harder for thieves to remove an airbag from the steering wheel? A Honda spokesperson, Lynn Sealey, told the Sun-Times that could backfire.</p><p>“As with any vehicle or valuable component theft, determined thieves are often willing to damage a vehicle to accomplish their criminal goals,” Sealy wrote in an email, “and thus hardening a component against theft may inadvertently increase the amount of damage to a vehicle.”</p><p>Arapovic said other manufacturers design airbags in a way that makes them even easier to remove than those in Honda vehicles, which he said have a screw on each side of the airbag compartment.</p><p>“Most of the airbags are just like a spring system that all you have to do to remove it is a pry tool that goes on the side of the steering wheel,” he said. “And literally, if you get the right position, it just pops out.”</p><h3>What’s being done to stop airbag theft</h3><p>Airbag thefts often go underreported, as victims do not always contact law enforcement.</p><p>The Sun-Times reached out to CPD to better understand how the department is combating airbag thefts but did not receive a response.</p><p>Former CPD Deputy Supt. Riccio said when he was in charge of the Counter-Terrorism Bureau six years ago before retiring, he oversaw the Licensing Unit’s work, which at the time included addressing airbag thefts.</p><p>Riccio described catching an airbag burglar as a “stroke of luck,” given how quickly the thefts occur.</p><p>Instead, police would perform randomized searches of auto repair shops. Once at a shop, officers would ask to see receipts for various car parts.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-2f0000" name="image-2f0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/83eb4fe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4497x2524+0+425/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3e%2Faa%2F79c1d277361188a73cd7a111d024%2Fchicago-violence-charges.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2e11a67/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4497x2524+0+425/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3e%2Faa%2F79c1d277361188a73cd7a111d024%2Fchicago-violence-charges.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59b7ad1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4497x2524+0+425/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3e%2Faa%2F79c1d277361188a73cd7a111d024%2Fchicago-violence-charges.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Chicago Police First Deputy Supt. Anthony Riccio speaks at a Sept. 23 press conference at CPD headquarters." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59b7ad1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4497x2524+0+425/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3e%2Faa%2F79c1d277361188a73cd7a111d024%2Fchicago-violence-charges.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2fb21a2/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4497x2524+0+425/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3e%2Faa%2F79c1d277361188a73cd7a111d024%2Fchicago-violence-charges.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/59b7ad1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/4497x2524+0+425/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F3e%2Faa%2F79c1d277361188a73cd7a111d024%2Fchicago-violence-charges.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Former Chicago Police Department First Deputy Superintendent Anthony Riccio speaks during a press conference in 2019 at CPD headquarters. During his time in charge of Counter-Terrorism, he oversaw the Licensing Unit’s work, which included addressing airbag thefts.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Ashlee Rezin Garcia/Chicago Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Police would cite the owner if receipts did not match up to the corresponding car parts or did not come from a reputable source, and the owner would then appear in court.</p><p>If stolen airbags were found at the shop, the owner would be arrested for possession of stolen property.</p><p>Riccio said that every case was different.</p><p>“Your first violation, you may just get a fine. The second or third, you may get your license suspended. … If you're a repeat offender, you might lose your license completely,” Riccio said. “That was the decision of the administrative judge.”</p><h3>Dangers of using a stolen airbag</h3><p>There are risks involved in using stolen or counterfeit airbags, even if it seems like they fit in the compartment.</p><p>"You want to have the airbags checked out to find out if they're actually the right airbag,” said Rosemary Shahan, president and founder of Consumers for Auto Reliability and Safety.</p><p>Shahan said a driver may not know if their airbags are stolen and defective until it's too late. For those who have gotten their airbags replaced, Shahan recommends having a professional ensure the airbags will work in an accident.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/48204f1/2147483647/strip/true/crop/8192x4598+0+433/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F10%2Ffd%2F97a570b741d7926f0db4777f8ff6%2Fairbagtheft-2605080105.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Beto Guerra, mechanic at Royce Auto, installs a new airbag in a Honda with a stolen airbag, in Bensenville, IL, on May 4, 2026. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>“The airbags are there to protect your head, your face, your spinal cord, your internal organs,” she said. “Even if you're wearing a seatbelt, it's not enough to protect you in a moderate to severe crash. You have to have the airbag there.”</p><p>Frontal airbags have saved over 50,000 lives in a 30-year period, according to the NHTSA.</p><h3>Protecting your car from airbag theft</h3><p>There is no way to completely stop someone from stealing airbags.</p><p>Shahan recommends people park in areas overnight with street lights and cameras and take advantage of private garages if possible.</p><p>“The Club,” a bar that locks over a car steering wheel, has become popular, and <a class="Link" href="https://www.instagram.com/p/DXrbCGJlYg1/" target="_blank" ><u>some CPD districts</u></a> have given them away for free at community events.</p><p>Shahan said a determined thief can cut right through the bars and steal the airbag, but Degenaars said The Club has been a great deterrent since he got his car back with its replaced airbag.</p><p>“For the time being, that seems to be the best option in terms of trying to stop it from happening again,” he said.</p><div class="Enhancement" entries.author: Zoe Singer

entries.title: Mujer del área de Chicago recibe trasplante de riñón gracias a una lectora del Sun-Times tras años de búsqueda
entries.summary: <p>Hace un año, Christine Hernández y Eileen Kerlin Walsh eran totalmente desconocidas. Ahora, Hernández espera algún día visitar a la familia de Kerlin Walsh en Irlanda.</p><p>Hernández, de 51 años, había pasado los últimos ocho años haciendo todo lo posible por encontrar un donante de riñón, desde repartir tarjetas de presentación con su historia hasta mostrar su búsqueda en letreros a lo largo de las carreteras. Se encontraba entre más de 3,700 personas en Illinois que esperaban un trasplante de riñón.</p><p>Kerlin Walsh leyó sobre los esfuerzos de Hernández el otoño pasado en un <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2025/10/17/a-campaign-to-find-a-new-kidney-spans-social-media-billboards-and-tv-this-chicago-woman-is-still-searching" target="_blank" >reportaje del Chicago Sun-Times</a>. Le impresionó la idea de que Hernández buscaba un milagro y que aún mantenía la esperanza a pesar de haber pasado tantos contratiempos.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-350000" name="module-350000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“Y solo pensé, ‘Qué oportunidad, qué privilegio ser el milagro de alguien’”, dijo Kerlin Walsh.</p><p>Así que decidió donarle un riñón a Hernández. El trasplante se llevó a cabo en abril.</p><p>Hernández ve la conexión que ahora tiene con Kerlin Walsh como una “hermandad de por vida”.</p><p>A veces, las personas le dan un apodo a su órgano donado. Hernández decidió llamar a su riñón “Milagro”.</p><p>“No sabía si alguna vez tendría la oportunidad de tener una vida de nuevo”, dijo Hernández, “y poder disfrutar de la vida como antes”.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Christine Hernández había buscado durante mucho tiempo y en diferentes lugares para encontrar un donante de riñón vivo.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Zubaer Khan / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘Me enfrenté a la realidad’</h3><p>La madre fallecida de Kerlin Walsh creía en los milagros. Antes de su muerte en 2021, las dos habían hecho peregrinaciones a lugares de sanación, desde Roma hasta el Santuario de Knock en Irlanda. Los viajes eran una forma de que Kerlin Walsh pasara tiempo con su madre. Ser una de 12 hijos significaba que no tenía mucho tiempo a solas con ella.</p><p>Ella piensa que su madre jugó un papel en su decisión de convertirse en donante de riñón.</p><p>“Nunca sentí que estuviera a la altura de su fe”, dijo mientras se le llenaban los ojos de lágrimas. “Algo sobre esto… me da la oportunidad de tal vez ser la hija de mi madre”.</p><div class="Enhancement" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Eileen Kerlin Walsh sostiene una foto de su madre, Mary Kerlin, cuyas lecciones la inspiraron a donar su riñón.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Eso no fue todo lo que convenció a Kerlin Walsh para convertirse en la donante de Hernández. Tenían el mismo tipo de sangre. Ambas eran madres con hijos de edades similares.</p><p>Y leyó sobre Hernández y lo que estaba pasando un mes después de cumplir 60 años. Ese logro la llevó a reflexionar sobre su propia vida. Hizo un video sobre sus logros y sobre cómo quería pasar la próxima parte de su vida retribuyendo.</p><p>“Lo vi y lo volví a ver y realmente me pregunté qué querían decir con eso”, dijo. “Me enfrenté a la realidad”.</p><h3>Más desafíos que encontrar un donante</h3><p>Kerlin Walsh contactó a UI Health en octubre para postularse como donante de riñón para Hernández. Eventualmente recibió una carta en febrero confirmando que era compatible. Le contó a su esposo y sus hijos sobre su decisión, pero se reservó decírselo a otros.</p><p>Encontrar un donante era solo uno de los obstáculos de Hernández. Tenía una rara forma de enfermedad renal, MUC1. Y tenía niveles extremadamente altos de anticuerpos, lo que aumentaba el riesgo de que su cuerpo pudiera rechazar un órgano donado.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9549685/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3024x1697+0+1167/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8c%2Fab%2F3a6fa30c4ec898bdbc7efe2f2b46%2Fkidney-260510-02.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Después de años de espera por un trasplante de riñón, Christine Hernández se está recuperando en casa tras la cirugía realizada en abril.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Los médicos usaron un medicamento llamado Imlifidase que ha ayudado a pacientes como Hernández a someterse con éxito a un trasplante al descomponer los anticuerpos. El fármaco ha sido aprobado para su uso en Europa, el Reino Unido y Australia. Pero su fabricante, la empresa sueca Hansa Biopharma, todavía está en proceso de obtener la aprobación de la Administración de Alimentos y Medicamentos de Estados Unidos (FDA).</p><p>El Dr. Lorenzo Gallon, director del Programa de Trasplante de Órganos Abdominales de UI Health, dijo que tomó alrededor de tres meses conseguir dos frascos del medicamento a través de un proceso conocido como uso compasivo. Gallon dijo que cree que Hernández es la primera persona en Illinois en recibir un trasplante con Imlifidase.</p><h3>‘La amé instantáneamente’</h3><p>Alrededor de una semana antes del trasplante, Kerlin Walsh estaba sentada en la sala de espera de UI Health cuando escuchó que llamaban el nombre de Hernández. No se habían conocido antes.</p><p>“Esta señora frente a mí simplemente se acerca y se aleja, y mi corazón estaba latiendo con fuerza”, dijo Kerlin Walsh. “Y pensé, ‘Oh, Dios mío, oh, Dios mío, oh, Dios mío, es ella. Está en frente a mí. ¿Debería decirle algo?’”</p><p>Le preguntó a una enfermera si los donantes y los receptores normalmente se conocían. La enfermera dijo que no suele ser así, a menos que suceda por accidente. Kerlin Walsh le dijo a la enfermera que había visto a Hernández en la sala de espera. La enfermera habló con Hernández y luego las reunió.</p><p>Hernández y Kerlin Walsh se abrazaron. Lloraron. Hablaron sobre sus familias. Intercambiaron números de teléfono. Las mujeres comenzaron a enviarse mensajes ocasionales.</p><p>“Le amé instantáneamente y sé que ella también me amó”, dijo Kerlin Walsh.</p><p>Los médicos administraron uno de los frascos de Imlifidase el 9 de abril y los preocupantes anticuerpos ya no pudieron detectarse, dijo Gallon. Eso permitió una breve ventana para realizar el trasplante.</p><p>Hernández oró con sus hijos antes de la cirugía, preocupada por no salir bien.</p><p>“Fue aterrador”, dijo. “Vas a entrar a esta gran cirugía, sabiendo que estaba en alto riesgo. Pero iba a seguir adelante. Solo puse toda mi fe en Dios y en mis médicos, confiaba en ellos”.</p><p>Kerlin Walsh dijo que la realidad no se asentó hasta unos 10 minutos antes de la cirugía.</p><p>“Sólo empecé a llorar porque el miedo realmente se apoderó de mí y luego también el alivio”, dijo Kerlin Walsh. “Porque no quería salir del hospital ese día sin haber hecho esta donación”.</p><h3>‘Nunca perdí mi fe’</h3><p>Casi un mes después de la operación, Hernández está de vuelta en casa en River Grove. Lleva mascarillas para minimizar la exposición a cualquier cosa que pudiera afectar su recuperación. Aún asiste al hospital para chequeos cada dos días.</p><p>Podría tomar de tres a seis meses antes de que se la considere recuperada. En un año, podrá comenzar a pensar en viajar fuera de los Estados Unidos, dijo Hernández.</p><p>Gallon dijo que Hernández está siendo monitoreada de cerca y que está “casi fuera de peligro”.</p><div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Eileen Kerlin Walsh, quien donó un riñón a Christine Hernández, se está recuperando en su hogar en Oak Lawn.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Anthony Vazquez / Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Kerlin Walsh también está en casa ahora, en Oak Lawn, recuperándose de la cirugía. Los días después de la operación, dijo, fueron un poco dolorosos, pero poco a poco volvió a sentirse como ella misma. Tomó tiempo libre del trabajo para recuperarse, aunque planeó dedicar parte de ese tiempo a trabajar en su libro.</p><p>“Me di cuenta de que este era el capítulo final de mi libro y esta era mi última historia de milagros, peregrinaciones, fe, familia y mi tributo a mi madre”, dijo.</p><p>Hernández está esperanzada de que pronto se sentirá lo suficientemente bien como para tomarse un café con Kerlin Walsh y conocerla mejor. Quiere tomarse unas vacaciones con sus hijos, quizás visitar a la familia en Puerto Rico o ir a Disneyland. Está ansiosa por convertirse en una defensora de quienes buscan donar órganos.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a Fueron a su restaurante peruano favorito para celebrar su cumpleaños. Uno de sus hijos le regaló una pintura que retrata el momento en que Kerlin Walsh se preocupó por ella después del trasplante. En ella, las mujeres se miran a los ojos, tomándose de las manos.</p><p><i>Traducido con una herramienta de inteligencia artificial (AI) y editado por</i> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/la-voz" target="_blank" ><i>La Voz Chicago</i></a></p>
entries.author: Elvia Malagón

entries.title: John Prine, one of the most influential songwriters of the era, to be feted with star-studded Chicago tribute
entries.summary: <p>Chicago will host a large fall celebration in honor of musician John Prine, who began building his iconic career as a singer-songwriter in the city’s folk scene.</p><p>Announced Monday, the Prine family and an extended family of musicians will honor the beloved musician on Oct. 8 with a tribute concert at the Chicago Theatre called “Souvenirs: 80 Years of John Prine.” timed to the late performer’s 80th birthday two days later.</p><p>If there was something John Prine loved as much as music, it was birthdays, said his wife, Fiona Prine, explaining why she couldn’t let his 80th come and go this fall without a proper fete. “Birthdays were huge for John, his own and everybody else's. He celebrated everyone with abundance. It was just a part of his wonderful attitude towards life.”</p><p>The Chicago event will come six years after the Illinois native unexpectedly passed away from COVID complications in 2020, and organizers, including Old Town School of Folk Music, have curated a lineup reflective of his legacy. Expect more events to be announced around the date.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-4a0000" name="image-4a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8f88a7a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5616x3152+0+296/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F12%2F0b9c2ed54c278fba1e99fce8d9b9%2Fjohn-prine-photo-by-rett-rogers.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/90d4f0f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5616x3152+0+296/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F12%2F0b9c2ed54c278fba1e99fce8d9b9%2Fjohn-prine-photo-by-rett-rogers.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8bc601a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5616x3152+0+296/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F12%2F0b9c2ed54c278fba1e99fce8d9b9%2Fjohn-prine-photo-by-rett-rogers.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="John Prine - Photo by Rett Rogers.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8bc601a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5616x3152+0+296/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F12%2F0b9c2ed54c278fba1e99fce8d9b9%2Fjohn-prine-photo-by-rett-rogers.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/3f772d5/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5616x3152+0+296/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F12%2F0b9c2ed54c278fba1e99fce8d9b9%2Fjohn-prine-photo-by-rett-rogers.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8bc601a/2147483647/strip/true/crop/5616x3152+0+296/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F41%2F12%2F0b9c2ed54c278fba1e99fce8d9b9%2Fjohn-prine-photo-by-rett-rogers.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>The Maywood-born Prine influenced a generation of singer-songwriters.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Photo by Rett Rogers </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-950000" name="module-950000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><b>SOUVENIRS: 80 YEARS OF JOHN PRINE</b><br>When: Oct. 8<br>Where: Chicago Theatre, 175 N. State St.<br>Tickets: Presale May 13; onsale May 15 <a class="Link" href="https://www.ticketmaster.com/event/070064A57623192E" target="_blank" >via Ticketmaster</a><br></div> </div><p>Hosted by actor/musician John C. Reilly, the October concert will bring together a who’s who of Americana music, including Steve Earle, Amos Lee, Josh Ritter, Margo Price and Joy Oladokun. Several acts with Chicago ties will perform including <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/02/06/chicago-indie-rockers-ratboys-release-latest-album-singing-to-an-empty-chair" >Ratboys,</a> <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2024/08/29/shemekia-copeland-blame-it-on-eve-chicago-blues" >Shemekia Copeland,</a> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/music/2026/03/19/hurray-for-the-riff-raff-new-album-alynda-segarra-live-forever-chicago-new-orleans" >Hurray for the Riff Raff’s Alynda Segarra</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/art/2024/02/22/jon-langford-art-exhibit-tony-fitzpatricks-studio-chicago" >Jon Langford.</a></p><p>The musicians will perform selections from Prine’s 18-album songbook while backed by his long-time band. Tickets go on sale this Friday, May 15 with presales beginning Wednesday, May 13.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-950000" name="image-950000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/b5f1474/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1924x1080+236+0/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F1e%2Ff01ff17c409dbfbb352df3b994e0%2Fsouvenirs-banner.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/1ab7714/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1924x1080+236+0/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F1e%2Ff01ff17c409dbfbb352df3b994e0%2Fsouvenirs-banner.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8939138/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1924x1080+236+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F1e%2Ff01ff17c409dbfbb352df3b994e0%2Fsouvenirs-banner.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Souvenirs - Banner.jpg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8939138/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1924x1080+236+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F1e%2Ff01ff17c409dbfbb352df3b994e0%2Fsouvenirs-banner.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/0f9160f/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1924x1080+236+0/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F1e%2Ff01ff17c409dbfbb352df3b994e0%2Fsouvenirs-banner.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8939138/2147483647/strip/true/crop/1924x1080+236+0/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fee%2F1e%2Ff01ff17c409dbfbb352df3b994e0%2Fsouvenirs-banner.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> </figure> </div> </div><p>The event takes its name from Prine’s 1972 song “Souvenirs,” which harkens back to his humble career origins. Born in 1946 in Maywood to a family with Kentucky roots, Prine was working as a mail carrier in the western suburb when he started crafting lyrics on his routes about the everyday people and commonplace life he’d come across.</p><p>By night, he had a presence at the Old Town School in Lincoln Park, and that gig spilled over to the nearby folk club Fifth Peg. After taking a friend’s dare to perform on that stage during an amateur night, Prine unexpectedly found himself with a regular set and needed more material.</p><p>“The club owner was like, ‘We're going to give you a job.’ And John thought, I can't get up there and sing the same songs again because it's probably going to be the same people. So, he literally wrote ‘Souvenirs’ while driving from his home in Maywood down to the city,” Fiona recounted. “It’s a beautiful song, and there's a lot of resonance to that word. Not to mention, he was somebody who literally bought souvenirs everywhere he went. My house is full of them, real and metaphorical, so it felt like the right name for this event.”</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-hangRight> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-f70000" name="image-f70000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/2c8a16d/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+185/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff4%2Fdd%2F1d27120343dd8de43f194e9b74ba%2Ffiona-whelan-prine-headshot.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/52bd50c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+185/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff4%2Fdd%2F1d27120343dd8de43f194e9b74ba%2Ffiona-whelan-prine-headshot.jpeg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d2a7ffe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+185/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff4%2Fdd%2F1d27120343dd8de43f194e9b74ba%2Ffiona-whelan-prine-headshot.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Fiona Whelan Prine Headshot.jpeg" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d2a7ffe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+185/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff4%2Fdd%2F1d27120343dd8de43f194e9b74ba%2Ffiona-whelan-prine-headshot.jpeg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/037b044/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+185/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff4%2Fdd%2F1d27120343dd8de43f194e9b74ba%2Ffiona-whelan-prine-headshot.jpeg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d2a7ffe/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3600x2020+0+185/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Ff4%2Fdd%2F1d27120343dd8de43f194e9b74ba%2Ffiona-whelan-prine-headshot.jpeg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Fiona Prine is helping organize the concert. </p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Provided </p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>The right place also had to be Chicago, she says. While there have been tributes in other cities, like the star-studded 2022 event “You’ve Got Gold: A Celebration of John Prine” in Nashville (also released as a concert film), it only felt right to go back to Prine’s roots for this 80th birthday bash, said Fiona, and to fill it with a good number of fellow homegrown talents.</p><p>“John was always drawn to Illinois, he had a very close affinity to it, and we definitely wanted to have Illinois represented,” Fiona says of intentionally settling on a largely local talent pool. One of those acts is indie rock group <a class="Link" href="https://www.wbez.org/music/2026/02/06/chicago-indie-rockers-ratboys-release-latest-album-singing-to-an-empty-chair" target="_blank" ><u>Ratboys.</u></a> Singer/guitarist Julia Steiner wrote WBEZ/Sun-Times over email that John Prine “ was simply the best storyteller on the planet, and that legacy lives on and shines bright through his songs. We can’t wait to be a part of this celebration, to play one of our favorite Prine songs and enjoy so many others and pay tribute to a man who’ll inspire us forever.”</p><p>Proceeds from the Oct. 8 event will benefit the Hello In There Foundation, a nonprofit that the Prine family established in 2021 to continue giving back to the communities John long lifted up. To date, the foundation has given nearly $1.5 million in grants to veterans, immigrants, refugees and those needing support for food and housing insecurity, mental health challenges and addiction recovery.</p><p>“Our mission is based on how John operated in the world,” said Fiona. “I think part of why John still resonates today is that he was so uninterested in fame or in celebrity, but he did believe in his music. And every song he ever wrote showed you who he is and how he empathized with people, whether it's a veteran in ‘Sam Stone’ or a frustrated housewife in ‘Angel From Montgomery.’</p><p>“While he didn't expect a whole lot, I think he would be very pleased to know that he has left a legacy that has kept us all going.”</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div storytelling</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p></p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-140000" entries.title: Airbag thefts are on the rise
entries.summary: <h3>Good morning, Chicago. ✶</h3><p>🔎 <b>Below</b>: Thieves are increasingly targeting airbags in Chicago. We explain why.</p><p>🗞️ <b>Plus</b>: City work benefits the Obama Presidential Center, a Mother's Day event for incarcerated people and more news you need to know.</p><p>📝 <b>Keeping score</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-j/" target="_blank" >The Cubs fell to the Rangers</a>, 3-0; <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-t/" target="_blank" >the White Sox beat the Mariners</a>, 2-1; <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-i/" target="_blank" >the Stars lost to the KC Current</a>, 3-0.</p><p>📧 <b>Subscribe</b>: <a class="Link" href="https://newsletter-hub.suntimes.com/" target="_blank" >Get this newsletter delivered to your inbox weekday mornings.</a></p><p>⏱️: <i>An 8-minute read</i></p><hr><p></p><h4>TODAY’S WEATHER ☀️</h4><p>Sunny with a high near 58</p><hr><p></p><h4>TODAY’S TOP STORY 🔎</h4><div class="Enhancement" class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p> A Honda whose airbag was stolen waits to be repaired at Royce Auto in Bensenville.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Manuel Martinez/WBEZ</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-h/" target="_blank" >Why are airbag thefts increasing in Chicago?</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-k/" target="_blank" >Zoe Singer</a></h5><p><b>Inflated thefts</b>: There has been an uptick in car break-ins targeting airbags across the Chicago area, according to experts and an analysis of Chicago Police Department community alerts. </p><p><b>Recall and resale</b>: Demand for stolen airbags increased nationwide following the recall of approximately 67 million of them that began a decade ago, according to the National Highway Traffic Safety Administration. It takes some burglars less than one minute to break into a car and steal the airbag from the driver’s seat, to eventually sell on the black market. </p><p><b>Hot air:</b> The Lake View area has the highest number of airbag theft community alerts from December 2025 to April 2026: 151. The second-highest number, 18, occurred in Humboldt Park. The alerts don’t capture every crime, as airbag thefts often go underreported.</p><p><b>What can be done?</b>: There is no way to completely stop someone from stealing airbags. The Sun-Times reached out to Chicago police to better understand how the department is combating the thefts but did not receive a response. One auto safety group recommends that people park overnight in areas with street lights and cameras, though one police officer described catching an airbag burglar as a "stroke of luck."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-u/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>LET’S HEAR FROM YOU 🗣️</h4><p><i>Has your vehicle's airbag been stolen? How have you tried to prevent future thefts?</i></p><p><i>Respond with your first and last name and neighborhood. href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-o/" target="_blank" >Hundreds gather for funeral honoring Chicago police officer slain at hospital</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-b/" target="_blank" >Cindy Hernandez</a> and <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-n/" target="_blank" >Kade Heather</a></h5><p><b>Officer mourned</b>: Slain Chicago police Officer John Bartholomew was remembered Friday as "truly loved by all" as hundreds of mourners — among them police, officials and loved ones — gathered at St. Andrew’s Greek Orthodox Church in Edgewater to say their farewells.</p><p><b>Key context</b>: Bartholomew was shot and killed April 25 at Endeavor Health Swedish Hospital. A robbery suspect he and his partner had brought to the hospital is accused of opening fire inside a CT room, killing Bartholomew and critically wounding the partner. That officer is improving but has "a long way to go" in recovery, Chicago’s top cop said Friday.</p><p><b>Key quote</b>: James Bartholomew said losing his brother was like "being frozen in time." He said: "He wasn’t only my brother, he was my best friend. And now he’s gone ... If you knew him, you knew his heart was full. His heart was pure."</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-p/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>POLITICS ✶</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-c40000" name="image-c40000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/438fd1c/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6125x3438+0+321/resize/490x275!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fdd%2F84cf2da243b09061491b351602c5%2Fobamacost-260414-23.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/9e972cb/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6125x3438+0+321/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fdd%2F84cf2da243b09061491b351602c5%2Fobamacost-260414-23.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/faf015e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/6125x3438+0+321/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F1a%2Fdd%2F84cf2da243b09061491b351602c5%2Fobamacost-260414-23.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="The Obama Presidential Center campus at 6001 S Stony Island Ave in Jackson Park, Wednesday, April 8, 2026. portion</b>: When the Obama Presidential Center opens next month and its funders are honored and congratulated, these major financial contributors are worthy of a bow: Chicago and Illinois taxpayers.</p><p><b>Price tag</b>: The Chicago Department of Transportation said it had spent $123.3 million since 2022 on capital projects aimed at remaking roadways and green space in Jackson Park and around the center. href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-tl/" target="_blank" >Speed puzzlers battle at the Jigsaw Nationals competition</a></h3><p></p><h5>By <a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-tr/" target="_blank" >Erica Thompson</a></h5><p>As a first-time spectator at a speed puzzle competition, Cody Busic did not have high expectations. But when the contest started at Clarendon Fieldhouse in Uptown on Saturday morning, he was on the edge of his seat, breathlessly recounting the event like a seasoned announcer.</p><p>"No, this was actually thrilling," said Busic, who was there to support one of the competitors. "The clock starts, everyone’s silent. They’re all racing fast as hell, moving their little hands trying to flip over the pieces. There’s focus, there’s strategy and there’s a lot of movement, oddly enough, for people who look like they’re standing still."</p><p>It was an accurate description of the two-hour USA Jigsaw Puzzle Association solo competition, hosted by the Chicago Puzzle Club. Fifty participants opened their drawstring bags to reveal identical 500-piece puzzles depicting a San Francisco cable car traversing a sunlit California Street.</p><p>Some sat and some stood, hovering over white tables littered with drinks, puzzle trays and plush animal toys for good luck. Many donned headphones that played everything from Broadway tunes to classical music.</p><p>Ultimately Rae Paul, of Forest Park, was victorious in the solo contest, completing her puzzle in 53 minutes and 15 seconds.</p><p><a class="Link" href="https://newsletters.suntimes.com/t/t-l-wddhhjy-l-ty/" target="_blank" ><b>READ MORE</b></a></p><hr><p></p><h4>PICTURE CHICAGO 📸</h4><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a entries.title: City Council panel backs tax incentives for United Center entertainment district
entries.summary: <p>Chicago taxpayers should help bankroll development of a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/real-estate/2026/03/23/united-center-owners-wirtz-reinsdorf-1901-project-55-million-property-tax-break" >“cultural and entertainment district”</a> around the West Side’s United Center, a key city panel said Monday.</p><p>The City Council’s Committee on Economic, Capital and Technology Development voted unanimously to provide $54.7 million in tax breaks for the first phase of the massive development planned by the owners of the Bulls and the Blackhawks. The measure still needs full City Council approval.</p><p>The $7 billion “1901 Project” is meant to transform surface parking lots around the United Center into an entertainment district with a 6,000-seat music hall, public parks, a hotel and residential units.</p><p>The tax abatement — which would be applied over 12 years — would be the first infusion of public funding for the privately-owned stadium and came as “a surprise to some of us,” said Ald. Nicole Lee (11th), since it wasn’t part of the initial announcement of the project.</p><p>The tax relief would come in the form of a 7(b) incentive for the first phase, which allows the property to be assessed at a lower percentage than it would be otherwise.</p><p>That type of incentive is only for projects that would not be viable without the tax assistance, and where the project is located in an economically depressed area in need of development. The measure advanced Monday also requires the developers to invest $500 million into the first phase.</p><p>The measure was approved despite some pushback from members concerned about diversity, who were angered to learn that none of the construction firms tapped for the first phase of the project thus far are Latino-owned.</p><p>In response, a lawyer representing the owners of the United Center vowed that “there will be Latino… participation in this project.”</p><p>“We haven't started construction yet, and so we haven't identified some of those sub[contractors],” said Katie Jahnke Dale, with the firm DLA Piper. “But I, just, none of us here have the answer on how the first three [contractors] were selected.”</p><p>Committee chair Derrick Curtis (18th) said that questions about representation should be answered before he calls the measure for a full council vote.</p><p>Mayor Brandon Johnson introduced the tax assistance plan in March and has defended it since, even as the city remains cash-strapped and Johnson has railed against tax incentives being considered at the state level for the Chicago Bears and a <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/bears-stadium/2026/05/11/bears-stadium-arlington-heights-hammond-indiana-what-to-know" >proposed stadium</a> in Arlington Heights.</p><p>Johnson said in March that the project would bring much needed jobs and development to the West Side.</p><p>“This is a project that is going to create thousands of jobs and opportunities for the people across the city, but particularly for development on the West Side,” Johnson told reporters.</p><p>The project will be the first major development in the area in decades.</p><p>It will be overseen by freshman Ald. Walter “Red” Burnett (27th) who succeeded his father last year and grew up near the United Center.</p><p>“When people think about West Loop and Fulton Market, they assume that the United Center, Near West Side, is a part of that. It's not the case. We've seen more buildings deconstructed west of Ashland than we have in any other part on the West Side of Chicago,” Burnett said.</p><p>“I remember personally going to Popeyes on Ashland and Madison growing up, because that was the closest restaurant that we had. That was demolished 10 years ago.”</p>
entries.author: Mariah Woelfel | WBEZ

entries.title: Cook County judge puts off ruling on whether to install special prosecutor to probe Operation Midway Blitz
entries.summary: <p>A Cook County judge questioned prosecutors Monday<b> </b>about whether <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/02/19/chicago-ice-crime-border-patrol-trump-midway-blitz-alex-pretti-renee-good-eileen-oneill-burke" target="_blank" >protocols they announced this year</a> for the potential prosecution of Operation Midway Blitz agents actually ruled out charges for nonviolent crimes such as conspiracy<b> </b>or perjury.</p><p>Prosecutors denied setting any limits for themselves. But Cook County Circuit Judge Erica<b> </b>Reddick<b> </b>posed the question while considering whether to <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/03/12/chicago-cook-county-oneill-burke-trump-ice-border-patrol-midway-blitz-special-prosecutor" target="_blank" >appoint a special prosecutor</a> to investigate those federal agents — offering possible insight into her thinking.</p><p>Reddick did not rule Monday on whether to appoint a special prosecutor, despite previously <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/04/24/chicago-trump-midway-blitz-crime-ice-border-patrol-oneill-burke" target="_blank" >setting that goal for herself</a>. The judge said she now expects to rule on May 21.</p><p>The decision she hands down could turn out to be a crucial turning point in efforts to hold agents responsible for their conduct during the deportation campaign.</p><p>A coalition of elected officials, clergy, journalists and attorneys say a special prosecutor should be appointed because<b> </b>Cook County State’s Attorney Eileen<b> </b>O’Neill<b> </b>Burke<b> </b>has turned a “blind eye” to “egregious acts of violence” by federal agents.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2026/04/24/chicago-trump-midway-blitz-crime-ice-border-patrol-oneill-burke" >Ruling on whether to appoint a special prosecutor to probe Midway Blitz agents expected in coming weeks</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>But O’Neill Burke’s office points to a <a class="Link" href="https://www.illinoiscourts.gov/resources/bb31d7b0-e90c-4843-8486-c740ae2624ef/file" target="_blank" >2017 Illinois Supreme Court decision</a> addressing the power of a state’s attorney to investigate alleged crimes. Her team says its role is to “support”<b> </b>law enforcement investigations, not to be the “primary investigators.”</p><p>“The state’s attorney has repeatedly condemned the tactics used by the Trump administration and during Operation Midway Blitz, denouncing the abhorrent conduct we have all witnessed," the office said in a statement following Monday’s hearing.</p><p>Reddick delayed her ruling after attorneys on both sides of the issue filed supplemental briefs with the court. The coalition seeking a special prosecutor shared the recent <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/politics/2026/04/30/pritzker-illinois-accountability-commission-operation-midway-blitz-trump-immigration" target="_blank" >Illinois Accountability Commission report</a>, which <a class="Link" href="https://ilac.illinois.gov/content/dam/soi/en/web/ilac/documents/final-report/IAC-Final-Report-April-30-2026.pdf" target="_blank" >found that</a> federal agents regularly used force against Illinois residents “without justification”<b> </b>during Midway Blitz.</p><p>Gov JB Pritzker assembled the commission to investigate the agents' conduct during the deportation campaign.</p><p>O’Neill Burke’s office followed up with its own filing Friday. It pointed to its support of the recently initiated <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/05/05/ice-midway-blitz-chicago-trump-silverio-villegas-gonzalez" target="_blank" >Illinois State Police investigation</a> into the September killing of<b> </b><a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/immigration/2025/09/15/silverio-villegas-gonzalez-fatally-shot-by-ice-agents-touched-the-lives-of-so-many-loved-ones-say" target="_blank" >Silverio<b> </b>Villegas<b> </b>Gonzalez</a><b> </b>by a U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent<b> </b>in Franklin Park.</p><p>Coalition attorneys declined to answer questions Monday about whether that investigation should affect the judge’s decision. They said they’ll address it in another brief due to the judge Friday.</p><p>The coalition includes the Chicago News Guild, which represents journalists at the Chicago Sun-Times.</p><p>Reddick, the presiding judge of the criminal division, had questions for O’Neill Burke’s team Monday about the protocol released by her office in February. It outlined how her office would work with state and local law enforcement when “a death, shooting, act of violence, or use of force incident related to federal immigration enforcement activity occurs.”</p><p>The coalition’s list of crimes potentially committed by immigration agents also includes nonviolent crimes.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/crime/2026/05/05/ice-midway-blitz-chicago-trump-silverio-villegas-gonzalez" >Illinois State Police investigating deadly ICE shooting of Silverio Villegas González in Chicago suburb</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>So Reddick asked about the scope of O’Neill Burke’s protocol and whether her office had limited itself to pursuing charges only for allegations of violence. The judge specifically brought up the potential for conspiracy or perjury charges.</p><p>“Will the scope — the stated limit of the scope — preclude the state from pursuing a prosecution — if the facts support it — of the conspiracy charge?” Reddick asked.</p><p>Yvette<b> </b>Loizon, the chief assistant state’s attorney for policy and external affairs, said the protocol set no such limit.</p><p>“We wanted to make sure that we were giving law enforcement clear direction on how to access the state’s attorney’s office in those matters,” Loizon explained.</p><p><i>Neither the reporter nor editors who worked on this story — including some represented by the News Guild — have been involved in the litigation described in this article.</i></p>
entries.author: Jon Seidel

entries.title: Battle continues over Illinois law to limit credit card fees
entries.summary: <p>A battle over credit card transaction fees continues after a federal appeals court on Friday sent the case about a new Illinois law seeking to limit so-called swipe fees back to District Court.</p><p>When customers use credit cards, businesses typically pay credit card companies an interchange fee on each transaction, including sales tax and workers’ tips, that ranges from about 2% to 4%.</p><p>In 2024, Illinois passed a first-in-the-nation law to end credit card fees on sales tax and tips that businesses pay. The Interchange Fee Prohibition Act was set to take effect July 1. But banking associations sued the state. A federal judge upheld parts of the law in February, but the plaintiffs appealed. </p><p>Oral arguments were scheduled for Wednesday but were canceled when the 7th Circuit U.S. Court of Appeals sent the case back last week to a lower court.</p><p>“This is standard operating procedure given the credit card companies’ attempt to insert a new issue at the appellate level,” Rob Karr, CEO of the Illinois Retail Merchants Association, said in a statement on Friday.</p><p>The plaintiffs, including the Illinois Bankers Association, said in a statement on Friday, “We welcome the opportunity to resume our legal challenge to the Illinois Interchange Fee Prohibition Act in district court.” They argued it “conflicts with federal law, and recent regulatory actions only reaffirm that fact.”</p><p>The case was sent back to consider a recent order from the U.S. Office of the Comptroller of the Currency — part of the U.S. Department of the Treasury. In April, the OCC’s order exempted national banks from the law but not Illinois credit unions and community banks.</p><p>“Banks and credit card companies will stop at nothing to protect their ability to charge outrageous swipe fees, going so far as to recruit the Trump administration to block an Illinois law that was recently upheld by a federal judge,” Karr said in an April statement.</p><p>The Electronic Payments Coalition, which represents Visa, Mastercard and banks, applauded the OCC’s order in a statement. The lobby group called for Illinois to repeal the law, saying it “introduces significant uncertainty in the marketplace, where some credit and debit cards may work and others may not.”</p><p>It’s unclear whether the law will go into effect as scheduled.</p><p>Ben Jackson, executive vice president of the Illinois Bankers Association, said the law will disrupt customer transactions, cause confusion and burden small businesses with upgrading payment systems.</p><p>Karr, of the Illinois Retail Merchants Association, said there will be “chaos” only if credit card companies and banks "intentionally" cause it.</p><p>He said credit card companies can comply with the law since they already manage different taxes in every state. “They have to do all of that. It’s moving a percentage from one line to another line,” Karr said.</p><p>If the law takes effect, 2Bears Tavern Group, which owns five North Side bars, doesn’t expect to change its payment system because it already delineates fee information clearly.</p><p>“The law could be implemented without issue, delay or cost,” Mark Robertson, 2Bears’ president, said.</p><div class="Enhancement" alt="A man stands in front of a wall covered with mirrors and framed posters" srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/80ab2fd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F0e%2F6bbf399146e99bda0dcaca952639%2Fswipe-260511-40.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/e6bbced/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F0e%2F6bbf399146e99bda0dcaca952639%2Fswipe-260511-40.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/80ab2fd/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+158/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2F8e%2F0e%2F6bbf399146e99bda0dcaca952639%2Fswipe-260511-40.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>Mark Robertson, co-owner of 2Bears Tavern Group, at his company’s Marty’s Martini Bar in Andersonville.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p></p><h3>‘The money is not ours’</h3><p>As the legal battle over the law continues, many small businesses deal with credit card fees every day.</p><p>Minyoli, a family-owned Taiwanese restaurant in Andersonville, stopped letting customers split the bill with multiple credit cards. The business gets charged a percentage of the bill, as well as a flat fee for each swipe.</p><p>“With already razor-thin margins in the restaurant business, swipe fees have a huge financial impact,” Minyoli General Manager X Wang said. “Since almost 100% of our transactions are credit card based, this essentially becomes a permanent cost for the restaurant.”</p><p>Swipe fees are yet another challenge for Potash Markets, which struggles with soaring inflation and stiff competition from supermarket giants. The 75-year-old family business has three grocery stores in the Gold Coast.</p><p>Swipe fees are “a hindrance that adds to the cost of doing business. It factors into the prices we charge,” Art Potash, owner of Potash Markets, said. “If more customers knew we are paying fees on taxes and it contributes to the cost of their groceries, I think they'd be in favor of the law.”</p><p>Potash said it’s unfair retailers pay credit card fees on sales tax that goes to the state.</p><p>“We’re doing something for the state of Illinois. We’re collecting the tax for them. It’s not our money,” he said. “We do the right thing and get penalized. It makes sense that Illinois would want to make it right.”</p><p>As more customers use credit cards, swipe fees are a growing expense for business owners.</p><p>A decade ago, credit card payments were less than half of the transactions at 2Bears, which owns Marty’s Martini Bar in Andersonville. Today, credit card payments are nearly 99% of sales, Robertson said. This year, 2Bears expects to pay more than $200,000 in credit card fees.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-0a0000" name="image-0a0000"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275" 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/47d0b15/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+148/resize/980x550!/format/webp/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F6a%2F44ae0dbf45a0a38cb7aa645826b3%2Fswipe-260511-50.jpg 2x" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <source width="490" height="275" data-srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d29d0e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+148/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F6a%2F44ae0dbf45a0a38cb7aa645826b3%2Fswipe-260511-50.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" srcset="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" /> <img class="Image" alt="Customers sit at the bar inside Marty's Martini Bar in Andersonville." srcset="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d29d0e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+148/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F6a%2F44ae0dbf45a0a38cb7aa645826b3%2Fswipe-260511-50.jpg 1x,https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/d0170aa/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+148/resize/980x550!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F6a%2F44ae0dbf45a0a38cb7aa645826b3%2Fswipe-260511-50.jpg 2x" width="490" height="275" data-src="https://cst.brightspotcdn.com/dims4/default/8d29d0e/2147483647/strip/true/crop/3000x1684+0+148/resize/490x275!/quality/90/?url=https%3A%2F%2Fchorus-production-cst-web.s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com%2Fbrightspot%2Fdf%2F6a%2F44ae0dbf45a0a38cb7aa645826b3%2Fswipe-260511-50.jpg" data-lazy-load="true" src="data:image/svg+xml;base64,PHN2ZyB4bWxucz0iaHR0cDovL3d3dy53My5vcmcvMjAwMC9zdmciIHZlcnNpb249IjEuMSIgaGVpZ2h0PSIyNzVweCIgd2lkdGg9IjQ5MHB4Ij48L3N2Zz4=" > </picture> <div class="Figure-content"><figcaption class="Figure-caption"><p>2Bears Tavern Group President Mark Robertson stands behind the bar at Marty’s Martini Bar in Andersonville.</p></figcaption><span class="line"></span><div class="Figure-credit"><p>Tyler Pasciak LaRiviere/Sun-Times</p></div></div> </figure> </div> </div><p>Businesses often pay credit card fees on workers’ tips. They're allowed to deduct the fees from workers’ tips, though 2Bears doesn’t do that.</p><p>“The money is not ours. We are just the conduit to other parties, our employees and the government,” Robertson said.</p><p>But he points out with wages for <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/work/2026/03/26/mayor-johnson-veto-freezing-tipped-minimum-wage-divides-restaurant-industry" target="_blank" >tipped workers</a> rising rapidly, businesses like his may have to consider deducting swipe fees from tips.</p><p>Credit card fees are “essentially totally uncontrollable other than passing them on to customers or not accepting card payments,” Robertson said. “More and more businesses are choosing to pass on those costs to customers by charging a fee at checkout.”</p><p>Robertson emphasized that <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2025/02/05/chicago-restaurants-charging-extra-eggs-bird-flu-soaring-costs" target="_blank" >high inflation</a> has been unrelenting since the COVID-19 <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/small-business/2025/03/21/restaurants-covid-pandemic-two-fish-crab-blind-faith-five-years-struggling" target="_blank" >pandemic</a>. Meanwhile, customers seem to be cutting back on spending.</p><p>Those pressures, combined with “a regulatory environment in Chicago that is decidedly unfriendly to small businesses in the last several years, makes for a less than optimistic view for the economy locally,” Robertson said.</p><p>Illinois’ swipe fee law “was one of the few positives that have come out of state and local government for the hospitality industry in the last six years,” he said.</p><p>Many small businesses like 2Bears are “reaching a breaking point with inflationary pressures,” Robertson said. “This law is attempting to draw a line in the sand around these costs.”</p>
entries.author: Amy Yee

entries.title: Supreme Court temporarily extends women’s access to a widely used abortion pill
entries.summary: <p>WASHINGTON — The Supreme Court is leaving women’s access to a widely used abortion pill untouched until at least Thursday, while the justices consider whether to allow restrictions on the drug, mifepristone, to take effect.</p><p>Justice Samuel Alito’s order Monday allows women seeking abortions to continue obtaining the pill at pharmacies or through the mail, without an in-person visit to a doctor. It prevents restrictions on mifepristone imposed by a federal appeals court from taking effect for the time being.</p><p>The court is dealing with its latest abortion controversy four years after its conservative majority overturned Roe v. Wade and allowed more than a dozen states to effectively ban abortion outright.</p><p>The case before the court stems from a lawsuit Louisiana filed to roll back the Food and Drug Administration’s rules on how mifepristone can be prescribed. The state claims the policy undermines the ban there, and it questions the safety of the drug, which was first approved in 2000 and has repeatedly been deemed safe and effective by FDA scientists.</p><p>Lower courts concluded that Louisiana is likely to prevail, and a three-judge panel of the 5th U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/05/01/court-restricts-abortion-access-blocking-mailing-mifepristone" target="_blank" >ruled that mail access and telehealth visits should be suspended</a> while the case plays out.</p><p>The drug is most often used for abortion in combination with another drug, misoprostol. Medication abortions accounted for nearly two-thirds of all abortions in the U.S. in 2023, the last year for which statistics are available.</p><div class="RelatedList Enhancement" data-module data-align-center> <div class="RelatedList-title">Related</div> <ul class="RelatedList-items"> <li class="RelatedList-items-item"> <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/health/2026/05/04/supreme-court-restores-access-to-abortion-pill-mifepristone-through-telehealth-mail-and-pharmacies" target="_blank" >Supreme Court restores access to abortion pill mifepristone through telehealth, mail and pharmacies</a> </li> </ul> </div> <p>The current dispute is similar to one that reached the court three years ago.</p><p>Lower courts then also sought to restrict access to mifepristone, in a case brought by physicians who oppose abortion. They filed suit in the months after the court overturned Roe.</p><p>The Supreme Court blocked the 5th Circuit ruling from taking effect over the dissenting votes of Alito and Justice Clarence Thomas. Then, in 2024, the high court unanimously dismissed the doctors’ suit, reasoning they did not have the legal right, or standing, to sue.</p><p>In the current dispute, mainstream medical groups, the pharmaceutical industry and Democratic members of Congress have weighed in cautioning the court against limiting access to the drug. Pharmaceutical companies said a ruling for abortion opponents would upend the drug approval process.</p><p>The FDA has eased a number of restrictions initially placed on the drug, including who can prescribe it, how it is dispensed and what kinds of safety complications must be reported.</p><p>Despite those determinations, abortion opponents have been challenging the safety of mifepristone for more than 25 years. They have filed a series of petitions and lawsuits against the agency, generally alleging that it violated federal law by overlooking safety issues with the pill.</p><p>President Donald Trump’s administration has been unusually quiet at the Supreme Court. It declined to file a written brief recommending what the court should do, even though federal regulations are at issue.</p><p>The case puts Trump’s Republican administration in a difficult place. Trump has relied on the political support of anti-abortion groups but has also seen ballot question and poll results that show Americans generally support abortion rights.</p><p>Both sides took the silence as an implicit endorsement of the appellate ruling. Alito is both the justice in charge of handling emergency appeals from Louisiana and the author of the 2022 decision that declared abortion is not a constitutional right and returned the issue to the states.</p>
entries.author: AP

entries.title: Beneficiarios de DACA en Chicago enfrentan retrasos en las renovaciones y preocupaciones de ser deportados
entries.summary: <p>Melanny B. está a punto de celebrar un logro.</p><p>Pero en lugar de conmemorar su título de maestría en educación superior con familiares y amigos, planea esconderse en el lugar más seguro que conoce: su casa.</p><p>Melanny, de 31 años, llegó al área de Chicago desde Ecuador cuando sólo tenía un año. Es una de las miles de personas indocumentadas que fueron traídas a los Estados Unidos cuando eran niños y están esperando sus renovaciones de dos años para el programa de Acción Diferida para los Llegados en la Infancia, o DACA.</p><div class="RichTextSidebarModule Enhancement" data-module data-align-center><a class="AnchorLink" id="module-ba0000" name="module-ba0000"></a> <div class="RichTextModule-items RichTextBody"><h3>La Voz Chicago WhatsApp</h3>Encuentra más noticias en nuestro canal de WhatsApp. <a class="Link" href="https://www.whatsapp.com/channel/0029Vb0XT1XCHDylm3rCs40P" target="_blank" ><b>Síguenos.</b></a></div> </div><p>“En un momento en que debería estar celebrando, en un momento en que esto es un gran logro, estoy un poco atrapada en casa, segura y sin ponerme en peligro”, dijo a Chicago Sun-Times, hablando bajo la condición de no ser identificada por temor a su seguridad.</p><p>Los beneficiarios de DACA, a menudo llamados "Dreamers", están experimentando grandes retrasos, a veces superiores a seis meses, en las renovaciones. Mientras tanto, algunos han perdido sus empleos.</p><p>Los beneficiarios de DACA y los defensores de la inmigración ven la demora como una forma de forzar a las personas a salir del programa que el presidente Donald Trump ha intentado sofocar desde su primer mandato.</p><p>“Cada día, me despierto con mucha ansiedad, mucha depresión", dijo Melanny. "Mi sistema nervioso ha estado realmente fuera de control desde noviembre de [2024]. Y cada día es algo nuevo, con DACA siempre en el limbo. Es algo que se está acumulando constantemente y es un peso muy pesado y agotador que he llevado durante los últimos 12 años de mi vida”.</p><p>Según los Servicios de Ciudadanía e <a class="Link" href="https://chicago.suntimes.com/inmigracion" target="_blank" >Inmigración</a> de Estados Unidos (USCIS) el 80% de las renovaciones de DACA, en promedio, están tardando cuatro meses en completarse. Pero existen retenciones de procesamiento no especificadas que se están aplicando a solicitantes de ciertos países, según el National Immigration Law Center. Otros retrasos se atribuyen a citas biométricas que fueron suspendidas durante la pandemia.</p><p>El camino para las personas indocumentadas que fueron traídas a los Estados Unidos como niños comenzó bajo el presidente Barack Obama en 2012, y ahora hay más de 830,000 personas que se han convertido en Dreamers. En Illinois, había 28,330 personas con estatus activo de DACA en junio de 2024, según el National Immigration Forum.</p><p>Trump atacó el programa durante su primer mandato, poniendo fin a las solicitudes de nuevos beneficiarios de DACA. Ha habido una plétora de batallas legales, y en 2021 un juez federal continuó una prohibición de nuevos beneficiarios de DACA.</p><p>Junto con los retrasos en las renovaciones, nuevos datos proporcionados a miembros del Congreso muestran que el Servicio de Control de Inmigración y Control de Aduanas (ICE) arrestó a 261 beneficiarios de DACA a nivel nacional entre el 1 de enero de 2025 y el 19 de noviembre de 2025, de los cuales 86 fueron deportados.</p><p>El último golpe al programa llegó el mes pasado, cuando la Junta de Apelaciones de Inmigración del Departamento de Justicia emitió una decisión que establece que la autorización de DACA no puede proteger a los beneficiarios de los procedimientos de deportación, preparando el camino para más deportaciones.</p><div class="Enhancement" data-align-center> <div class="Enhancement-item" data-crop=""> <figure class="Figure"><a class="AnchorLink" id="image-435109" name="image-435109"></a> <picture data-crop="medium"> <source type="image/webp" width="490" height="275"
